Country in the Horn of Africa

This article is about the country. For other uses, see [Ethiopia (disambiguation)](/wiki/Ethiopia_(disambiguation) "Ethiopia (disambiguation)"). For the genus of moths, see [Ethopia](/wiki/Ethopia "Ethopia").

This article contains several [patronymic](/wiki/Patronymic "Patronymic") names rather than family names. **Ethiopia**[[b]](#cite_note-17), officially the **Federal Democratic Republic of Ethiopia**, is a [landlocked country](/wiki/Landlocked_country "Landlocked country") located in the [Horn of Africa](/wiki/Horn_of_Africa "Horn of Africa") region of [East Africa](/wiki/East_Africa "East Africa"). It shares borders with [Eritrea](/wiki/Eritrea "Eritrea") to the north, [Djibouti](/wiki/Djibouti "Djibouti") to the northeast, [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia") to the east, [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya") to the south, [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan") to the west, and [Sudan](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan") to the northwest. Ethiopia covers a land area of 1,104,300 square kilometres (426,400 sq mi).[[16]](#cite_note-18) As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ethiopia&action=edit), it has around 135 million inhabitants, making it the [14th-most populous](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population "List of countries and dependencies by population") country.[[17]](#cite_note-:10-19) The national capital and largest city, [Addis Ababa](/wiki/Addis_Ababa "Addis Ababa"), lies several kilometres west of the [East African Rift](/wiki/East_African_Rift "East African Rift") that splits the country into the [African](/wiki/African_Plate "African Plate") and [Somali](/wiki/Somali_Plate "Somali Plate") tectonic plates.[[18]](#cite_note-CIA2-20)

[Anatomically modern humans](/wiki/Early_modern_human "Early modern human") emerged from modern-day Ethiopia.[[19]](#cite_note-21)[[20]](#cite_note-22)[[21]](#cite_note-23)[[22]](#cite_note-24)[[23]](#cite_note-25) In 980 BC, the Kingdom of [D'mt](/wiki/D%CA%BFmt "Dʿmt") extended its realm over Eritrea and the northern region of Ethiopia, while the [Kingdom of Aksum](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Aksum "Kingdom of Aksum") maintained a unified civilization in the region for 900 years. Christianity arrived to the kingdom in 330 AD,[[24]](#cite_note-26) while Islam influenced during the [first Hijra](/wiki/Migration_to_Abyssinia "Migration to Abyssinia") in 615.[[25]](#cite_note-27) After the collapse of Aksum in 960, the [Zagwe dynasty](/wiki/Zagwe_dynasty "Zagwe dynasty") ruled the north-central parts of Ethiopia. The [Shewan](/wiki/Shewa "Shewa") usurper, [Yekuno Amlak](/wiki/Yekuno_Amlak "Yekuno Amlak"), overthrew the dynasty in 1270 at the [Battle of Ansata](/wiki/Battle_of_Ansata "Battle of Ansata"), inaugurating the [Ethiopian Empire](/wiki/Ethiopian_Empire "Ethiopian Empire") and the [Solomonic dynasty](/wiki/Solomonic_dynasty "Solomonic dynasty") – asserting its lineage through Aksumite legitimacy by the biblical [Solomon](/wiki/Solomon "Solomon") and [Queen of Sheba](/wiki/Queen_of_Sheba "Queen of Sheba"), and [Menelik I](/wiki/Menelik_I "Menelik I") was the first emperor. By the 14th century, the empire had grown in prestige through territorial expansion and fighting against adjacent territories; most notably, the [Ethiopian–Adal War](/wiki/Ethiopian%E2%80%93Adal_War "Ethiopian–Adal War") (1529–1543) contributed to fragmentation of the empire, which ultimately fell under a decentralization known as *[Zemene Mesafint](/wiki/Zemene_Mesafint "Zemene Mesafint")* in the mid-18th century. [Emperor](/wiki/Emperor_of_Ethiopia "Emperor of Ethiopia") [Tewodros II](/wiki/Tewodros_II "Tewodros II") ended *Zemene Mesafint* at the beginning of his reign in 1855, marking the reunification and modernization of Ethiopia.[[26]](#cite_note-28)

Since the late 19th century, it resisted foreign invasions and [expanded its territory](/wiki/Menelik_II%27s_conquests "Menelik II's conquests") under Emperor [Menelik II](/wiki/Menelik_II "Menelik II"), remaining independent during the [Scramble for Africa](/wiki/Scramble_for_Africa "Scramble for Africa") and forming its current borders. Ethiopia lost its sovereignty in 1936, when [Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") under [fascism](/wiki/Italian_fascism "Italian fascism") invaded and annexed it with former colony of [Eritrea](/wiki/Italian_Eritrea "Italian Eritrea") and [Somaliland](/wiki/Italian_Somaliland "Italian Somaliland"), later forming [Italian East Africa](/wiki/Italian_East_Africa "Italian East Africa"). In 1941, during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), it was [occupied by the British Army](/wiki/East_African_campaign_(World_War_II) "East African campaign (World War II)"), and its full sovereignty was [restored in 1944](/wiki/Anglo-Ethiopian_Agreement#Negotiating_a_new_agreement "Anglo-Ethiopian Agreement"). The [Derg](/wiki/Derg "Derg"), a Soviet-backed military junta, took power in 1974 after [deposing](/wiki/1974_Ethiopian_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1974 Ethiopian coup d'état") Emperor [Haile Selassie](/wiki/Haile_Selassie "Haile Selassie") and the Solomonic dynasty, and ruled the country for nearly 17 years amidst the [Ethiopian Civil War](/wiki/Ethiopian_Civil_War "Ethiopian Civil War"). Following the [dissolution of the Derg](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Derg "Fall of the Derg") in 1991, the [Ethiopian People's Revolutionary Democratic Front](/wiki/Ethiopian_People%27s_Revolutionary_Democratic_Front "Ethiopian People's Revolutionary Democratic Front") (EPRDF) dominated the country with a [new constitution](/wiki/1995_Constitution_of_Ethiopia "1995 Constitution of Ethiopia") and [ethnic-based federalism](/wiki/Ethnic_federalism#Ethiopia "Ethnic federalism"). Since then, Ethiopia has suffered from prolonged and unsolved [inter-ethnic clashes](/wiki/Ethnic_discrimination_in_Ethiopia "Ethnic discrimination in Ethiopia") and political instability marked by [authoritarianism](/wiki/Authoritarianism "Authoritarianism"). From 2018, regional and ethnically based factions carried out armed attacks in [multiple ongoing wars throughout Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopian_civil_conflict_(2018%E2%80%93present) "Ethiopian civil conflict (2018–present)").[[27]](#cite_note-BBC1120-29)

Ethiopia is a [multi-ethnic state](/wiki/Multinational_state "Multinational state") with over 80 different [ethnic groups](/wiki/List_of_ethnic_groups_in_Ethiopia "List of ethnic groups in Ethiopia"). [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") is the most widely professed faith in the country, with the largest denomination being the Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church, followed by [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Ethiopia "Islam in Ethiopia") and a small percentage of [traditional faiths](/wiki/Traditional_African_religion "Traditional African religion"). The country is a founding member of the UN, the headquarters of the [African Union Commission](/wiki/African_Union_Commission "African Union Commission"), and many other global [non-governmental organizations](/wiki/Non-governmental_organization "Non-governmental organization") focused on Africa, also becoming a full member of [BRICS](/wiki/BRICS "BRICS") in 2024.[[28]](#cite_note-30) Ethiopia is one of the [least developed countries](/wiki/Least_developed_countries "Least developed countries") but is sometimes considered an [emerging power](/wiki/Emerging_power "Emerging power"),[[29]](#cite_note-31)[[30]](#cite_note-32) having the [fastest economic growth](/wiki/List_of_African_countries_by_GDP_growth "List of African countries by GDP growth") in [sub-Saharan African](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa") countries because of [foreign direct investment](/wiki/Foreign_direct_investment "Foreign direct investment") in expansion of agricultural and manufacturing industries; [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Ethiopia "Agriculture in Ethiopia") is the country's largest economic sector, accounting for over 37% of the [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") as of 2022. Though the Ethiopian economy has experienced consistent growth, in terms of [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") and the [Human Development Index](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_Human_Development_Index "List of countries by Human Development Index") the country remains among the poorest in Africa.[[31]](#cite_note-33) Ethiopia faces numerous challenges, including high rates of [poverty](/wiki/Poverty_in_Ethiopia "Poverty in Ethiopia"), [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Ethiopia "Human rights in Ethiopia") violations, widespread [ethnic discrimination](/wiki/Ethnic_discrimination_in_Ethiopia "Ethnic discrimination in Ethiopia"), and a literacy rate of 60%.[[32]](#cite_note-34)[[33]](#cite_note-35)[[34]](#cite_note-:2-36)

## Etymology

See also: [Aethiopia](/wiki/Aethiopia "Aethiopia")

Tradition holds that the name Ethiopia (ኢትዮጵያ) comes from the name of the first King of Ethiopia, Ethiop, or [Ethiopis](/wiki/Ethiopis "Ethiopis").

Ayele Berkerie explains:

> According to an Ethiopian tradition, the term Ethiopia is derived from the word Ethiopis, a name of the Ethiopian king, the seventh in the ancestral lines. Metshafe Aksum or the Ethiopian Book of Aksum identifies Itiopis as the twelfth king of Ethiopia and the father of Aksumawi. The Ethiopians pronounce Ethiopia እትዮጵያ with a Sades or the sixth sound እ as in incorporate and the graph ጰ has no equivalent in English or Latin graphs. Ethiopis is believed to be the twelfth direct descendant of Adam. His father is identified as Kush, while his grandfather is known as Kam.[[35]](#cite_note-37)

In the 15th-century Ge'ez *[Book of Axum](/wiki/Book_of_Axum "Book of Axum")*, the name is ascribed to a legendary individual called *[Ityopp'is](/wiki/Ethiopis "Ethiopis")*. He was an extra-biblical son of [Cush, son of Ham](/wiki/Cush_(Bible) "Cush (Bible)"), said to have founded the city of [Axum](/wiki/Axum "Axum").[[36]](#cite_note-Agrvt-38)

The [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") name Αἰθιοπία (from Αἰθίοψ, "an Ethiopian") is a compound word, later explained as derived from the Greek words αἴθω and ὤψ (*eithō* "I burn" + *ōps* "face"). According to the [Liddell-Scott Jones Greek-English Lexicon](/wiki/A_Greek%E2%80%93English_Lexicon "A Greek–English Lexicon"), the designation properly translates as *burnt-face* in noun form and *red-brown* in adjectival form.[[37]](#cite_note-39) The historian [Herodotus](/wiki/Herodotus "Herodotus") used the appellation to denote those parts of Africa south of the [Sahara](/wiki/Sahara "Sahara") that were then known within the [Ecumene](/wiki/Ecumene "Ecumene") (habitable world).[[38]](#cite_note-Herodotus1-40) The earliest mention of the term is found in the works of [Homer](/wiki/Homer "Homer"), where it is used to refer to two people groups, one in Africa and one in the east from eastern Turkey to India.[[39]](#cite_note-41) This Greek name was borrowed into [Amharic](/wiki/Amharic "Amharic") as ኢትዮጵያ, *ʾĪtyōṗṗyā*.

In [Greco](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece")-[Roman](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") epigraphs, *Aethiopia* was a specific toponym for ancient [Nubia](/wiki/Nubia "Nubia").[[40]](#cite_note-Hatke-42) At least as early as c. 850,[[41]](#cite_note-43) the name *Aethiopia* also occurs in many translations of the [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament") in allusion to Nubia. The ancient [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language") texts identify Nubia instead as [Kush](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kush "Kingdom of Kush").[[42]](#cite_note-44) However, in the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament"), the Greek term Aithiops does occur, referring to a servant of the [Kandake](/wiki/Kandake "Kandake"), the queen of Kush.[[43]](#cite_note-45)

Following the Hellenic and biblical traditions, the [Monumentum Adulitanum](/wiki/Monumentum_Adulitanum "Monumentum Adulitanum"), a 3rd-century inscription belonging to the [Aksumite Empire](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Aksum "Kingdom of Aksum"), indicates that Aksum's ruler governed an area that was flanked to the west by the territory of Ethiopia and Sasu. The Aksumite King [Ezana](/wiki/Ezana_of_Axum "Ezana of Axum") eventually conquered Nubia the following century, and the Aksumites thereafter appropriated the designation "Ethiopians" for their own kingdom. In the [Ge'ez](/wiki/Ge%CA%BDez "Geʽez") version of the Ezana inscription, Aἰθίοπες is equated with the unvocalized *Ḥbšt* and *Ḥbśt* (Ḥabashat), and denotes for the first time the highland inhabitants of Aksum. This new [demonym](/wiki/Demonym "Demonym") was subsequently rendered as *ḥbs* ('Aḥbāsh) in [Sabaic](/wiki/Sabaean_language "Sabaean language") and as *Ḥabasha* in [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic").[[40]](#cite_note-Hatke-42) Derivatives of this are used in some languages that use loanwords from Arabic, for example in [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language") *Habsyah*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In English, and generally outside of Ethiopia, the country was historically known as [Abyssinia](/wiki/Abyssinia "Abyssinia"). This toponym was derived from the Latinized form of the ancient *Habash*.[[44]](#cite_note-46)

## History

Main article: [History of Ethiopia](/wiki/History_of_Ethiopia "History of Ethiopia")

Further information: [Ethiopian historiography](/wiki/Ethiopian_historiography "Ethiopian historiography")

### Prehistory

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Ge%27ez.svg/40px-Ge%27ez.svg.png)

**This article contains [Ethiopic text](/wiki/Ge%27ez_script "Ge'ez script").** Without proper [rendering support](/wiki/Help:Multilingual_support_(Ethiopic) "Help:Multilingual support (Ethiopic)"), you may see [question marks, boxes, or other symbols](/wiki/Specials_(Unicode_block)#Replacement_character "Specials (Unicode block)") instead of Ethiopic characters.

Main article: [Prehistoric Ethiopia](/wiki/Prehistoric_Ethiopia "Prehistoric Ethiopia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Homo_Sapiens_Idaltu.JPG/250px-Homo_Sapiens_Idaltu.JPG)](/wiki/File:Homo_Sapiens_Idaltu.JPG)

A *[Homo sapiens idaltu](/wiki/Herto_Man "Herto Man")* hominid skull

Several important finds have propelled Ethiopia and the surrounding region to the forefront of [palaeontology](/wiki/Paleontology "Paleontology"). The oldest [hominid](/wiki/Hominidae "Hominidae") discovered to date in Ethiopia is the 4.2 million-year-old *[Ardipithecus ramidus](/wiki/Ardipithecus "Ardipithecus")* ([Ardi](/wiki/Ardi "Ardi")) found by [Tim D. White](/wiki/Tim_D._White "Tim D. White") in 1994.[[45]](#cite_note-OldestHuman-47) The most well-known hominid discovery is *[Australopithecus afarensis](/wiki/Australopithecus_afarensis "Australopithecus afarensis")* ([Lucy](/wiki/Lucy_(hominid) "Lucy (hominid)")). Known locally as *Dinkinesh*, the specimen was found in the [Awash Valley](/wiki/Awash_Valley "Awash Valley") of [Afar Region](/wiki/Afar_Region "Afar Region") in 1974 by [Donald Johanson](/wiki/Donald_Johanson "Donald Johanson"), and is one of the most complete and best-preserved adult [Australopithecine](/wiki/Australopithecus "Australopithecus") fossils ever uncovered. Lucy's taxonomic name refers to the region where the discovery was made. This hominid is estimated to have lived 3.2 million years ago.[[46]](#cite_note-48)[[47]](#cite_note-Dinkinesh-49)[[48]](#cite_note-iho1-50)

Ethiopia is also considered one of the earliest sites of the emergence of [anatomically modern humans](/wiki/Early_modern_human "Early modern human"), *[Homo sapiens](/wiki/Human "Human")*. The oldest of these local fossil finds, the [Omo remains](/wiki/Omo_remains "Omo remains"), were excavated in the southwestern [Omo Kibish](/wiki/Omo_Kibish_Formation "Omo Kibish Formation") area and have been dated to the [Middle Paleolithic](/wiki/Middle_Paleolithic "Middle Paleolithic"), around 200,000 years ago.[[49]](#cite_note-Mcdougall2005-51) Additionally, skeletons of *[Homo sapiens idaltu](/wiki/Herto_Man "Herto Man")* were found at a site in the [Middle Awash](/wiki/Middle_Awash "Middle Awash") valley. Dated to approximately 160,000 years ago, they may represent an extinct subspecies of *Homo sapiens*, or the immediate ancestors of anatomically modern humans.[[50]](#cite_note-52) Archaic *Homo sapiens* fossils excavated at the [Jebel Irhoud](/wiki/Jebel_Irhoud "Jebel Irhoud") site in Morocco have since been dated to an earlier period, about 300,000 years ago,[[51]](#cite_note-53) while Omo-Kibish I (Omo I) from southern Ethiopia is the oldest anatomically modern *Homo sapiens* skeleton currently known (196 ± 5 kya).[[52]](#cite_note-54)

According to some anthropologists, the first [Afroasiatic](/wiki/Afroasiatic_languages "Afroasiatic languages")-speaking populations arrived in the region during the ensuing [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") era from the family's proposed [urheimat](/wiki/Afroasiatic_homeland "Afroasiatic homeland") ("original homeland") in the [Nile Valley](/wiki/Nile "Nile"),[[53]](#cite_note-Zarins-55) or the [Near East](/wiki/Near_East "Near East").[[54]](#cite_note-Aane-56) The majority of scholars today propose that the Afroasiatic family developed in northeast Africa because of the higher diversity of lineages in that region, a telltale sign of linguistic origin.[[55]](#cite_note-Blench143144-57)[[56]](#cite_note-58)[[57]](#cite_note-59)

In 2019, archaeologists discovered a 30,000-year-old [Middle Stone Age](/wiki/Middle_Stone_Age "Middle Stone Age") rock shelter at the [Fincha Habera](/wiki/Fincha_Habera,_Ethiopia "Fincha Habera, Ethiopia") site in [Bale Mountains](/wiki/Bale_Mountains "Bale Mountains") at an elevation of 3,469 metres (11,381 feet) above sea level. At this high altitude, humans are susceptible both to [hypoxia](/wiki/Hypoxemia#Environmental_oxygen "Hypoxemia") and to extreme weather. According to a study published in the journal *[Science](/wiki/Science_(journal) "Science (journal)")*, this dwelling is proof of the earliest permanent human occupation at high altitude yet discovered. Thousands of animal bones, hundreds of stone tools, and ancient fireplaces were discovered, revealing a diet that featured giant [mole rats](/wiki/Spalacidae "Spalacidae").[[58]](#cite_note-60)[[59]](#cite_note-61)[[60]](#cite_note-62)[[61]](#cite_note-63)[[62]](#cite_note-64)[[63]](#cite_note-65)[[64]](#cite_note-66)

Evidence of some of the earliest known stone-tipped projectile weapons (a characteristic tool of *Homo sapiens*), the stone tips of javelins or throwing spears, were discovered in 2013 at the Ethiopian site of [Gademotta](/wiki/Gademotta "Gademotta"), which date to around 279,000 years ago.[[65]](#cite_note-SahlePLOS1-67) In 2019, additional Middle Stone Age projectile weapons were found at Aduma, dated 100,000–80,000 years ago, in the form of points considered likely to belong to darts delivered by spear throwers.[[66]](#cite_note-Sahle-68)

### Antiquity

Main articles: [Dʿmt](/wiki/D%CA%BFmt "Dʿmt") and [Kingdom of Aksum](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Aksum "Kingdom of Aksum")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/KingEndybisEthiopia227-235CE.jpg/250px-KingEndybisEthiopia227-235CE.jpg)](/wiki/File:KingEndybisEthiopia227-235CE.jpg)

[Aksumite currency](/wiki/Aksumite_currency "Aksumite currency") of the [Aksumite king](/wiki/Axumite_kings "Axumite kings") [Endybis](/wiki/Endybis "Endybis"), 227–35, at the [British Museum](/wiki/British_Museum "British Museum")[[c]](#cite_note-69)

In 980 BC, [Dʿmt](/wiki/D%CA%BFmt "Dʿmt") was established in present-day [Eritrea](/wiki/Eritrea "Eritrea") and the northern part of Ethiopia in the Tigray region, and is widely believed to be the successor state to [Punt](/wiki/Land_of_Punt "Land of Punt"). This polity's capital was located at [Yeha](/wiki/Yeha "Yeha") in what is now northern Ethiopia. Most modern historians consider this civilization to be a native Ethiopian one, although in earlier times many suggested it was [Sabaean](/wiki/Sabaeans "Sabaeans")-influenced because of the latter's hegemony of the [Red Sea](/wiki/Red_Sea "Red Sea").[[67]](#cite_note-Munro-Hay57-70)

Other scholars regard Dʿmt as the result of a union of Afroasiatic-speaking cultures of the Cushitic and Semitic branches; namely, local [Agaw peoples](/wiki/Agaw_people "Agaw people") and Sabaeans from Southern Arabia. However, [Ge'ez](/wiki/Ge%CA%BDez "Geʽez"), the ancient Semitic language of Ethiopia, is thought to have developed independently from the [Sabaean language](/wiki/Sabaean_language "Sabaean language"). As early as 2000 BC, other Semitic speakers were living in Ethiopia and Eritrea where Ge'ez developed.[[68]](#cite_note-71)[[69]](#cite_note-72) Sabaean influence is now thought to have been minor, limited to a few localities, and disappearing after a few decades or a century. It may have been a trading or military colony in alliance with the Ethiopian civilization of Dʿmt or some other proto-Axumite state.[[67]](#cite_note-Munro-Hay57-70)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/The_Kingdom_of_Aksum.png/250px-The_Kingdom_of_Aksum.png)](/wiki/File:The_Kingdom_of_Aksum.png)

The [Empire of Axum](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Aksum "Kingdom of Aksum") at its peak in the 6th century

After the fall of Dʿmt during the 4th century BC, the Ethiopian plateau came to be dominated by smaller successor kingdoms. In the 1st century AD, the [Kingdom of Aksum](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Aksum "Kingdom of Aksum") emerged in what is now Tigray Region and Eritrea. According to the medieval *[Book of Axum](/wiki/Book_of_Axum "Book of Axum")*, the kingdom's first capital, Mazaber, was built by Itiyopis, son of Cush.[[36]](#cite_note-Agrvt-38) Aksum would later at times extend its rule into Yemen on the other side of the Red Sea.[[70]](#cite_note-73) The Persian prophet [Mani](/wiki/Mani_(prophet) "Mani (prophet)") listed Axum with Rome, Persia, and China as one of the four great powers of his era, during the 3rd century.[[71]](#cite_note-74) It is also believed that there was a connection between Egyptian and Ethiopian churches. There is faint evidence that the Aksumites were associated with the [Queen of Sheba](/wiki/Queen_of_Sheba "Queen of Sheba"), via their royal inscription.[[72]](#cite_note-:5-75)

Around 316 AD, [Frumentius](/wiki/Frumentius "Frumentius") and his brother Edesius from [Tyre](/wiki/Tyre,_Lebanon "Tyre, Lebanon") accompanied their uncle on a voyage to Ethiopia. When the vessel stopped at a Red Sea port, the natives killed all the travellers except the two brothers, who were taken to the court as [slaves](/wiki/Slavery_in_Ethiopia "Slavery in Ethiopia"). They were given positions of trust by the monarch, and they converted members of the royal court to Christianity. Frumentius became the first bishop of Aksum.[[73]](#cite_note-isbn0-313-32273-2-76) A coin dated to 324 shows that Ethiopia was the second country to officially adopt Christianity (after Armenia did so in 301), although the religion may have been at first confined to court circles; it was the first major power to do so. The Aksumites were accustomed to the Greco-Roman sphere of influence, but they embarked on significant cultural ties and trade connections between the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") and the Roman Empire via the [Silk Road](/wiki/Silk_Road "Silk Road"), primarily exporting [ivory](/wiki/Ivory_trade "Ivory trade"), tortoise shell, gold and emeralds, and importing silk and spices.[[72]](#cite_note-:5-75)[[74]](#cite_note-britishmuseum.org-77) The country's gold production in 2015 is 9 metric tons.[[75]](#cite_note-78)

### Middle Ages

Main article: [Ethiopia in the Middle Ages](/wiki/Ethiopia_in_the_Middle_Ages "Ethiopia in the Middle Ages")

The kingdom adopted the name "Ethiopia" during the reign of [Ezana](/wiki/Ezana_of_Axum "Ezana of Axum") in the 4th century. After the conquest of [Kingdom of Kush](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kush "Kingdom of Kush") in 330, the Aksumite territory reached its peak between the 5th and 6th centuries.[[67]](#cite_note-Munro-Hay57-70) This period was interrupted by several incursions into the South Arabian protectorate, including [Jewish](/wiki/Jews "Jews") [Dhu Nuwas](/wiki/Dhu_Nuwas "Dhu Nuwas") of the [Himyarite Kingdom](/wiki/Himyarite_Kingdom "Himyarite Kingdom") and the [Aksumite–Persian wars](/wiki/Aksumite%E2%80%93Persian_wars "Aksumite–Persian wars"). In 575, the Aksumites [besieged and retook Sana'a](/wiki/Sasanian_reconquest_of_Yemen "Sasanian reconquest of Yemen") following the assassination of its governor [Sayf ibn Dhī Yazan](/wiki/Sayf_ibn_Dhi_Yazan "Sayf ibn Dhi Yazan"). The Red Sea was left to the [Rashidun Caliphate](/wiki/Rashidun_Caliphate "Rashidun Caliphate") in 646, and the port city of [Adulis](/wiki/Adulis "Adulis") was plundered by Arab Muslims in the 8th century; along with irrevocable [land degradation](/wiki/Land_degradation "Land degradation"), claimed [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change_(general_concept) "Climate change (general concept)") and sporadic rainfall precipitation from 730 to 760,[[76]](#cite_note-79) these factors likely caused the kingdom to decline in power as part of an important trade route.[[67]](#cite_note-Munro-Hay57-70)[[77]](#cite_note-:12-80) Aksum came to an end in 960 when [Queen Gudit](/wiki/Gudit "Gudit") defeated the last king of Aksum.[[78]](#cite_note-81) In response, the remnant of the Aksumite population to shift into the southern region and establish the [Zagwe dynasty](/wiki/Zagwe_dynasty "Zagwe dynasty"), changing its capital to [Lalibela](/wiki/Lalibela "Lalibela").[[79]](#cite_note-82) Zagwe's rule ended when an [Amhara](/wiki/Amhara_people "Amhara people") noble man [Yekuno Amlak](/wiki/Yekuno_Amlak "Yekuno Amlak") revolted against King [Yetbarak](/wiki/Yetbarak "Yetbarak") and established the [Ethiopian Empire](/wiki/Ethiopian_Empire "Ethiopian Empire") (known by exonym "Abyssinia").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Bete_Medhane_Alem_in_Lalibela.JPG/250px-Bete_Medhane_Alem_in_Lalibela.JPG)](/wiki/File:Bete_Medhane_Alem_in_Lalibela.JPG)

[Biete Medhane Alem](/wiki/Biete_Medhane_Alem "Biete Medhane Alem"), built during [Zagwe dynasty](/wiki/Zagwe_dynasty "Zagwe dynasty") in the Middle Ages

The Ethiopian Empire initiated territorial expansion under the leadership of [Amda Seyon I](/wiki/Amda_Seyon_I "Amda Seyon I"). He launched campaigns against his Muslim adversaries to the east, resulting in a significant shift in the balance of power in favor of the Christians for the next two centuries. After Amda Seyon's successful eastern campaigns, most of the Muslim principalities in the [Horn of Africa](/wiki/Horn_of_Africa "Horn of Africa") came under the suzerainty of the Ethiopian Empire. Stretching from [Gojjam](/wiki/Gojjam "Gojjam") to the Somali Coast in [Zelia](/wiki/Zeila "Zeila").[[80]](#cite_note-83) Among these Muslim entities was the [Sultanate of Ifat](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Ifat "Sultanate of Ifat"). During the reign of Emperor [Zara Yaqob](/wiki/Zara_Yaqob "Zara Yaqob"), the Ethiopian Empire reached its pinnacle. His rule was marked by the consolidation of territorial acquisitions from earlier rulers, the oversight of the construction of numerous churches and monasteries, the active promotion of literature and art, and the strengthening of central imperial authority.[[81]](#cite_note-84)[[82]](#cite_note-:3b-85)[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConnelKillion2011160-86) Ifat's successor, the [Adal Sultanate](/wiki/Adal_Sultanate "Adal Sultanate"),[[84]](#cite_note-87) tried to conquer Ethiopia during the [Ethiopian–Adal War](/wiki/Ethiopian%E2%80%93Adal_War "Ethiopian–Adal War"), but was ultimately defeated at the 1543 [Battle of Wayna Daga](/wiki/Battle_of_Wayna_Daga "Battle of Wayna Daga").[[85]](#cite_note-88)

By the 16th century, an influx of migration by ethnic [Oromo](/wiki/Oromo_people "Oromo people") into northern parts of the region fragmented the empire's power. Embarking from present-day [Guji](/wiki/Guji_Zone "Guji Zone") and [Borena Zone](/wiki/Borena_Zone "Borena Zone"), the Oromos were largely motivated by several folkloric conceptions—beginning with *Moggaasaa*[[86]](#cite_note-89) and *Liqimssa*—many of whom related to their raids. This persisted until *gada* of Meslé.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPankhurst1997301-90)[[88]](#cite_note-:8-91) According to [Abba Bahrey](/wiki/Bahrey "Bahrey"), the earliest expansion occurred under Emperor [Dawit II](/wiki/Dawit_II "Dawit II") ([*luba* Melbah](/wiki/Luba_Melbah "Luba Melbah")), when they encroached to [Bale](/wiki/Bale_Province,_Ethiopia "Bale Province, Ethiopia") before invading Adal Sultanate.[[89]](#cite_note-Reference-92)

Ethiopia saw major diplomatic contact with Portugal from the 17th century, mainly related to religion. Beginning in 1555,[[90]](#cite_note-93) Portuguese Jesuits attempted to develop Roman Catholicism as the state religion. After several failures, they sent several missionaries in 1603, including the most influential, Spanish Jesuit [Pedro Paez](/wiki/Pedro_P%C3%A1ez "Pedro Páez").[[91]](#cite_note-94) Under Emperor [Susenyos I](/wiki/Susenyos_I "Susenyos I"), Roman Catholicism became the state religion of the Ethiopian Empire in 1622.[[92]](#cite_note-Parfitt2019-95) This decision caused an uprising by the Orthodox populace.[[93]](#cite_note-berndl2005-96)

### Early Modern Period (1632–1855)

Main articles: [Gondarine period](/wiki/Gondarine_period "Gondarine period") and [Zemene Mesafint](/wiki/Zemene_Mesafint "Zemene Mesafint")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/f/f4/Paris%2C_Biblioth%C3%A8que_nationale_de_France%2C_Ethiopien_d%27Abbadie_215%2C_folio_1r.JPEG/250px-Paris%2C_Biblioth%C3%A8que_nationale_de_France%2C_Ethiopien_d%27Abbadie_215%2C_folio_1r.JPEG)](/wiki/File:Paris,_Biblioth%C3%A8que_nationale_de_France,_Ethiopien_d%27Abbadie_215,_folio_1r.JPEG)

A copy of 17th century philosophical and [ethical](/wiki/Ethical "Ethical") treatise by [Ethiopian](/wiki/Ethiopian "Ethiopian") philosopher [Zara Yaqob](/wiki/Zara_Yaqob "Zara Yaqob")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Fasil_Ghebbi_IV_%2823869587160%29.jpg/250px-Fasil_Ghebbi_IV_%2823869587160%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fasil_Ghebbi_IV_(23869587160).jpg)

Fasil Ghebbi, one of the key castles of the [Gondarine period](/wiki/Gondarine_period "Gondarine period")

In 1632, Emperor [Fasilides](/wiki/Fasilides "Fasilides") halted Roman Catholic state administration, restoring [Orthodox Tewahedo](/wiki/Orthodox_Tewahedo "Orthodox Tewahedo") as the state religion.[[92]](#cite_note-Parfitt2019-95) Fasilides' reign solidified imperial power, relocating the capital to [Gondar](/wiki/Gondar "Gondar") in 1636, marking the beginning of the "[Gondarine period](/wiki/Gondarine_period "Gondarine period")".[[94]](#cite_note-97) He reclaimed lands, and exiled [Jesuits](/wiki/Jesuits "Jesuits") to [Fremona](/wiki/Fremona "Fremona"). During his rule, Fasilides constructed the iconic royal fortress, [Fasil Ghebbi](/wiki/Fasil_Ghebbi "Fasil Ghebbi"), built forty-four churches,[[95]](#cite_note-98) and revived [Ethiopian art](/wiki/Ethiopian_art "Ethiopian art"). He is also credited with building seven stone bridges over the [Blue Nile River](/wiki/Blue_Nile_River "Blue Nile River").[[96]](#cite_note-99)

Gondar's power declined after the death of [Iyasu I](/wiki/Iyasu_I "Iyasu I") in 1706. Following [Iyasu II](/wiki/Iyasu_II "Iyasu II")'s death in 1755, Empress [Mentewab](/wiki/Mentewab "Mentewab") brought her brother, [Ras](/wiki/Ras_(title) "Ras (title)") Wolde Leul, to Gondar, making him Ras Bitwaded. This led to regnal conflict between Mentewab's *Quaregnoch* and the Wollo group led by Wubit. In 1767, Ras [Mikael Sehul](/wiki/Mikael_Sehul "Mikael Sehul"), a regent in [Tigray Province](/wiki/Tigray_Province "Tigray Province"), seized Gondar, killing the child [Iyoas I](/wiki/Iyoas_I "Iyoas I") in 1769, the reigning emperor, and installed 70-year-old [Yohannes II](/wiki/Yohannes_II "Yohannes II").[[97]](#cite_note-100)

Between 1769 and 1855, Ethiopia witnessed the [Zemene Mesafint](/wiki/Zemene_Mesafint "Zemene Mesafint") or "Age of Princes," a period of isolation. Emperors became figureheads, controlled by regional lords and noblemen like *Ras* Mikael Sehul, Ras [Wolde Selassie](/wiki/Wolde_Selassie "Wolde Selassie") of Tigray, and by the [Yejju](/wiki/Yejju "Yejju") Oromo dynasty of the [Wara Sheh](/wiki/Zemene_Mesafint#Wara_Sheh_rulers_(Yejju)_(1784–1855) "Zemene Mesafint"), including Ras [Gugsa of Yejju](/wiki/Gugsa_of_Yejju "Gugsa of Yejju"). Before the Zemene Mesafint, Emperor Iyoas I had introduced the Oromo language (*Afaan Oromo*) at court, replacing Amharic.[[98]](#cite_note-101)[[99]](#cite_note-102)

### Age of Imperialism (1855–1916)

Main articles: [British expedition to Abyssinia](/wiki/British_expedition_to_Abyssinia "British expedition to Abyssinia"), [Menelik's Invasions](/wiki/Menelik%27s_Invasions "Menelik's Invasions"), [Egyptian–Ethiopian War](/wiki/Egyptian%E2%80%93Ethiopian_War "Egyptian–Ethiopian War"), [First Italo-Ethiopian War](/wiki/First_Italo-Ethiopian_War "First Italo-Ethiopian War"), and [Mahdist War](/wiki/Mahdist_War "Mahdist War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/T%C3%A9wodros_II_-_2.jpg/250px-T%C3%A9wodros_II_-_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:T%C3%A9wodros_II_-_2.jpg)

Emperor [Tewodros II](/wiki/Tewodros_II "Tewodros II") (r. 1855–1868) brought an end to Zemene Mesafint.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Menelik_-_Adoua-2_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Menelik_-_Adoua-2_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Menelik_-_Adoua-2_(cropped).jpg)

Emperor [Menelik II](/wiki/Menelik_II "Menelik II") defended Ethiopia's sovereignty during the age of imperialism.

Ethiopian isolationism ended following a British mission that concluded with an alliance between the two nations, but it was not until 1855 that the Amhara kingdoms of northern Ethiopia (Gondar, [Gojjam](/wiki/Gojjam "Gojjam"), and [Shewa](/wiki/Shewa "Shewa")) were briefly united after the power of the emperor was restored beginning with the reign of [Tewodros II](/wiki/Tewodros_II "Tewodros II").[[100]](#cite_note-Middleton2015-103)[[101]](#cite_note-104) Tewodros II began a process of consolidation, centralisation, and state-building that would be continued by succeeding emperors. This process reduced the power of regional rulers, restructured the empire's administration, and created a professional army. These changes created the basis for establishing the effective sovereignty and territorial integrity of the Ethiopian state.[[102]](#cite_note-105) In 1875 and 1876, Ottoman and Egyptian forces, accompanied by many European and American advisors, twice [invaded Abyssinia](/wiki/Egyptian%E2%80%93Ethiopian_War "Egyptian–Ethiopian War") but were initially defeated.[[103]](#cite_note-106) From 1885 to 1889 (under [Yohannes IV](/wiki/Yohannes_IV "Yohannes IV")), Ethiopia joined the [Mahdist War](/wiki/Mahdist_War "Mahdist War") allied to Britain, the Ottomans, and Egypt against the Sudanese Mahdist State. In 1887, [Menelik II](/wiki/Menelik_II "Menelik II"), king of [Shewa](/wiki/Shewa "Shewa"), invaded the [Emirate of Harar](/wiki/Emirate_of_Harar "Emirate of Harar") after his victory at the [Battle of Chelenqo](/wiki/Battle_of_Chelenqo "Battle of Chelenqo").[[104]](#cite_note-107) On 10 March 1889, Yohannes IV was killed by the Sudanese Khalifah Abdullah's army whilst leading his army in the [Battle of Gallabat](/wiki/Battle_of_Gallabat "Battle of Gallabat").[[105]](#cite_note-108)

Ethiopia, in roughly its current form, began under the reign of Menelik II, who was Emperor from 1889 until his death in 1913. From his base in the central province of Shewa, Menelik set out to annex territories to the south, east, and west[[106]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-109) — areas inhabited by the Oromo, [Sidama](/wiki/Sidama_people "Sidama people"), Gurage, [Welayta](/wiki/Welayta_people "Welayta people"), and other peoples.[[107]](#cite_note-igg-110) He achieved this with the help of *Ras* [Gobana Dacche](/wiki/Gobana_Dacche "Gobana Dacche")'s Shewan Oromo militia, which occupied lands that had not been held since [Ahmad ibn Ibrahim al-Ghazi](/wiki/Ahmad_ibn_Ibrahim_al-Ghazi "Ahmad ibn Ibrahim al-Ghazi")'s war, as well as other areas that had never been under Ethiopian rule.[[108]](#cite_note-111)

For his leadership, despite opposition from more traditional elements of society, Menelik II was heralded as a national hero. He had signed the [Treaty of Wuchale](/wiki/Treaty_of_Wuchale "Treaty of Wuchale") with Italy in May 1889, by which Italy would recognize Ethiopia's sovereignty so long as Italy could control an area north of Ethiopia (now part of modern Eritrea). In return, Italy was to provide Menelik with weapons and support him as emperor. The Italians used the time between the signing of the treaty and its ratification by the Italian government to expand their territorial claims. This [First Italo-Ethiopian War](/wiki/First_Italo-Ethiopian_War "First Italo-Ethiopian War") culminated in the [Battle of Adwa](/wiki/Battle_of_Adwa "Battle of Adwa") on 1 March 1896, in which Italy's colonial forces were defeated by the Ethiopians.[[107]](#cite_note-igg-110)[[109]](#cite_note-112) During this time, about a third of the population died in the [Great Ethiopian Famine](/wiki/Famines_in_Ethiopia "Famines in Ethiopia") (1888 to 1892),[[110]](#cite_note-113)[[111]](#cite_note-114) and the [rinderpest](/wiki/1890s_African_rinderpest_epizootic "1890s African rinderpest epizootic") swept through the area, destroying much of the herd economy. On 11 October 1897, Ethiopia adopted the [colours of the pan-African flag](/wiki/Pan-African_colours "Pan-African colours") with green, yellow and red stripes in representation of [pan-Africanist](/wiki/Pan-Africanist "Pan-Africanist") ideology.

### Haile Selassie I era (1916–1974)

Main articles: [Second Italo-Ethiopian War](/wiki/Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War "Second Italo-Ethiopian War"), [Italian Ethiopia](/wiki/Italian_Ethiopia "Italian Ethiopia"), [Italian East Africa](/wiki/Italian_East_Africa "Italian East Africa"), [Federation of Ethiopia and Eritrea](/wiki/Federation_of_Ethiopia_and_Eritrea "Federation of Ethiopia and Eritrea"), and [Eritrean War of Independence](/wiki/Eritrean_War_of_Independence "Eritrean War of Independence")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Ethiopian_Soldiers_Korean_War.jpg/250px-Ethiopian_Soldiers_Korean_War.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ethiopian_Soldiers_Korean_War.jpg)

Ethiopian troops sent by the government under the [Emperor Haile Selassie I](/wiki/Haile_Selassie "Haile Selassie") during the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War") fighting for South Korean independence

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Emperor_Haile_Selassie_I_with_President_FDR.jpg/250px-Emperor_Haile_Selassie_I_with_President_FDR.jpg)](/wiki/File:Emperor_Haile_Selassie_I_with_President_FDR.jpg)

[Emperor Haile Selassie I](/wiki/Haile_Selassie "Haile Selassie") with U.S. president [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") during the end of World War II

The early 20th century was marked by the reign of Emperor [Haile Selassie](/wiki/Haile_Selassie "Haile Selassie") (*Ras Tafari*). He came to power after [Lij Iyasu](/wiki/Lij_Iyasu "Lij Iyasu") was deposed, and undertook [a nationwide modernization campaign](/wiki/Modernization_under_Haile_Selassie "Modernization under Haile Selassie") from 1916 when he was made a *Ras* and Regent (*Inderase*) for the [Empress Regnant](/wiki/Queen_regnant "Queen regnant") [Zewditu](/wiki/Zewditu "Zewditu"), and became the *de facto* ruler of the Ethiopian Empire. Following Zewditu's death, on 2 November 1930, he succeeded her as emperor.[[112]](#cite_note-115) In 1931, Haile Selassie endowed Ethiopia with [its first constitution](/wiki/1931_Constitution_of_Ethiopia "1931 Constitution of Ethiopia") in emulation of Imperial Japan's [1890 Constitution](/wiki/Meiji_Constitution "Meiji Constitution").[[113]](#cite_note-116)
The independence of Ethiopia was interrupted by the [Second Italo-Ethiopian War](/wiki/Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War "Second Italo-Ethiopian War"), beginning when it was invaded by [Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy#Fascist_regime_(1922–1943) "Kingdom of Italy") in early October 1935, and by subsequent Italian rule of the country (1936–1941) after Italian victory in the war.[[114]](#cite_note-117) Italy, however, never managed to secure the country in its totality, due to resistance from the [Arbegnoch](/wiki/Arbegnoch "Arbegnoch"); this made Ethiopia, along with [Liberia](/wiki/Liberia "Liberia"), the only African countries to never be fully colonized.[[115]](#cite_note-118)[[116]](#cite_note-119) Following the entry of Italy into [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") forces, together with the Arbegnoch, liberated Ethiopia in the course of the [East African campaign](/wiki/East_African_campaign_(World_War_II) "East African campaign (World War II)") in 1941. The country was placed under [British military administration](/wiki/Occupied_Enemy_Territory_Administration_(Ethiopia) "Occupied Enemy Territory Administration (Ethiopia)"), and then Ethiopia's full [sovereignty](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty") was restored with the signing of the [Anglo-Ethiopian Agreement](/wiki/Anglo-Ethiopian_Agreement "Anglo-Ethiopian Agreement") in December 1944.[[117]](#cite_note-120)

On 24 October 1945, Ethiopia became a founding member of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"). In 1952, Haile Selassie orchestrated a [federation with Eritrea](/wiki/Federation_of_Ethiopia_and_Eritrea "Federation of Ethiopia and Eritrea"). He dissolved this in 1962 and annexed Eritrea, resulting in the [Eritrean War of Independence](/wiki/Eritrean_War_of_Independence "Eritrean War of Independence").[[118]](#cite_note-121) Haile Selassie also played a leading role in the formation of the [Organisation of African Unity](/wiki/Organisation_of_African_Unity "Organisation of African Unity") (OAU).[[119]](#cite_note-122) Opinion within Ethiopia turned against Haile Selassie, owing to the worldwide [1973 oil crisis](/wiki/1973_oil_crisis "1973 oil crisis") causing a sharp increase in gasoline prices starting on 13 February 1974, leading to student and worker protests.[[120]](#cite_note-123) The feudal oligarchical cabinet of [Aklilu Habte-Wold](/wiki/Aklilu_Habte-Wold "Aklilu Habte-Wold") was toppled, and a new government was formed with [Endelkachew Makonnen](/wiki/Endelkachew_Makonnen "Endelkachew Makonnen") serving as Prime Minister.[[121]](#cite_note-124)

### Derg era (1974–1991)

See also: [Ethiopian Revolution](/wiki/Ethiopian_Revolution "Ethiopian Revolution"), [Derg](/wiki/Derg "Derg"), [People's Democratic Republic of Ethiopia](/wiki/People%27s_Democratic_Republic_of_Ethiopia "People's Democratic Republic of Ethiopia"), [Ethiopian Civil War](/wiki/Ethiopian_Civil_War "Ethiopian Civil War"), [Red Terror](/wiki/Red_Terror_(Ethiopia) "Red Terror (Ethiopia)"), and [1983–1985 famine in Ethiopia](/wiki/1983%E2%80%931985_famine_in_Ethiopia "1983–1985 famine in Ethiopia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Mengistu_Haile_Mariam_3.jpg/250px-Mengistu_Haile_Mariam_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mengistu_Haile_Mariam_3.jpg)

[Mengistu Haile Mariam](/wiki/Mengistu_Haile_Mariam "Mengistu Haile Mariam") was sentenced to death [*in absentia*](/wiki/Trial_in_absentia "Trial in absentia") for committing [crimes during his rule](/wiki/Red_Terror_(Ethiopia) "Red Terror (Ethiopia)").

Haile Selassie's rule ended on 12 September 1974, when he was [deposed](/wiki/1974_Ethiopian_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1974 Ethiopian coup d'état") by the [Derg](/wiki/Derg "Derg"), a committee made up of military and police officers.[[122]](#cite_note-125) After the execution of [60 former government and military officials](/wiki/Massacre_of_the_Sixty "Massacre of the Sixty"),[[123]](#cite_note-126) the new [Provisional Military Administrative Council](/wiki/Provisional_Military_Administrative_Council "Provisional Military Administrative Council") abolished the monarchy in March 1975 and established Ethiopia as a [Marxist-Leninist state](/wiki/Marxist-Leninist_state "Marxist-Leninist state").[[124]](#cite_note-127) The abolition of [feudalism](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism"), increased [literacy](/wiki/Literacy "Literacy"), [nationalization](/wiki/Nationalization "Nationalization"), and sweeping [land reform](/wiki/Land_reform "Land reform") including the [resettlement and villagization](/wiki/Resettlement_and_villagization_in_Ethiopia "Resettlement and villagization in Ethiopia") from the [Ethiopian Highlands](/wiki/Ethiopian_Highlands "Ethiopian Highlands") became priorities.[[125]](#cite_note-128)

After a power struggle in 1977, [Mengistu Haile Mariam](/wiki/Mengistu_Haile_Mariam "Mengistu Haile Mariam") gained undisputed leadership of the Derg.[[126]](#cite_note-129) In 1977, Somalia, which had previously been receiving assistance and arms from the [USSR](/wiki/USSR "USSR"), invaded Ethiopia in the [Ogaden War](/wiki/Ogaden_War "Ogaden War"), capturing part of the [Ogaden](/wiki/Ogaden "Ogaden") region. Ethiopia recovered it after it began receiving massive military aid from the Soviet bloc countries.[[127]](#cite_note-130)[[128]](#cite_note-131)[[129]](#cite_note-132) By the end of the seventies, Mengistu presided over the second-largest army in all of [sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa"), as well as a formidable [air force](/wiki/Ethiopian_Air_Force "Ethiopian Air Force") and navy.

In 1976–78, up to 500,000 were killed as a result of the [Red Terror](/wiki/Red_Terror_(Ethiopia) "Red Terror (Ethiopia)"),[[130]](#cite_note-BBC-133) a violent [political repression](/wiki/Political_repression "Political repression") campaign by the Derg against various opposition groups.[[131]](#cite_note-Black_Book2-134)[[132]](#cite_note-135)[[133]](#cite_note-136) In 1987, the Derg dissolved itself and established the [People's Democratic Republic of Ethiopia](/wiki/People%27s_Democratic_Republic_of_Ethiopia "People's Democratic Republic of Ethiopia") (PDRE) upon the adoption of the [1987 Constitution of Ethiopia](/wiki/1987_Constitution_of_Ethiopia "1987 Constitution of Ethiopia").[[134]](#cite_note-137) A [1983–85 famine](/wiki/1983%E2%80%931985_famine_in_Ethiopia "1983–1985 famine in Ethiopia") affected around 8 million people, resulting in 1 million dead. Insurrections against [authoritarian](/wiki/Authoritarianism "Authoritarianism") rule sprang up, particularly in the northern regions of Eritrea and Tigray. The [Tigray People's Liberation Front](/wiki/Tigray_People%27s_Liberation_Front "Tigray People's Liberation Front") (TPLF) merged with other ethnically based opposition movements in 1989, to form the [Ethiopian People's Revolutionary Democratic Front](/wiki/Ethiopian_People%27s_Revolutionary_Democratic_Front "Ethiopian People's Revolutionary Democratic Front") (EPRDF).[[135]](#cite_note-138)

The collapse of [Marxism–Leninism](/wiki/Marxism%E2%80%93Leninism "Marxism–Leninism") during the [revolutions of 1989](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1989 "Revolutions of 1989") coincided with the Soviet Union stopping aid to Ethiopia altogether in 1990.[[136]](#cite_note-139)[[137]](#cite_note-140)[[138]](#cite_note-141) EPRDF forces advanced on Addis Ababa in May 1991, and Mengistu fled the country and was granted asylum in Zimbabwe.[[139]](#cite_note-142)[[140]](#cite_note-143)

### Federal Democratic Republic (1991–present)

Main article: [History of the Federal Democratic Republic of Ethiopia](/wiki/History_of_the_Federal_Democratic_Republic_of_Ethiopia "History of the Federal Democratic Republic of Ethiopia")

Further information: [Eritrean–Ethiopian border conflict](/wiki/Eritrean%E2%80%93Ethiopian_border_conflict "Eritrean–Ethiopian border conflict"), [Ethiopian civil conflict (2018–present)](/wiki/Ethiopian_civil_conflict_(2018%E2%80%93present) "Ethiopian civil conflict (2018–present)"), and [Tigray war](/wiki/Tigray_war "Tigray war")

See also: [1993 Eritrean independence referendum](/wiki/1993_Eritrean_independence_referendum "1993 Eritrean independence referendum")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Meles_Zenawi_-_World_Economic_Forum_on_Africa_2012.jpg/250px-Meles_Zenawi_-_World_Economic_Forum_on_Africa_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Meles_Zenawi_-_World_Economic_Forum_on_Africa_2012.jpg)

Meles Zenawi, a panelist at [World Economic Forum](/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum") on Africa 2012

In July 1991, the EPRDF convened a National Conference to establish the [Transitional Government of Ethiopia](/wiki/Transitional_Government_of_Ethiopia "Transitional Government of Ethiopia") composed of an 87-member [Council of Representatives](/wiki/Council_of_Representatives_of_Ethiopia "Council of Representatives of Ethiopia") and guided by a national charter that functioned as a transitional constitution.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELyons1996121–23-144) In 1994, a new constitution was written that established a parliamentary republic with a bicameral legislature and a judicial system.[[142]](#cite_note-Constitution2-145)

In April 1993, [Eritrea](/wiki/Eritrea "Eritrea") gained independence from Ethiopia after a [national referendum](/wiki/1993_Eritrean_independence_referendum "1993 Eritrean independence referendum").[[143]](#cite_note-146) In May 1998, a border dispute with Eritrea led to the [Eritrean–Ethiopian War](/wiki/Eritrean%E2%80%93Ethiopian_War "Eritrean–Ethiopian War"), which lasted until June 2000 and cost both countries an estimated $1 million a day.[[144]](#cite_note-147) This had a negative effect on Ethiopia's economy, and a [border conflict](/wiki/Eritrean%E2%80%93Ethiopian_border_conflict "Eritrean–Ethiopian border conflict") between the two countries would continue [until 2018](/wiki/2018_Eritrea%E2%80%93Ethiopia_summit "2018 Eritrea–Ethiopia summit").[[145]](#cite_note-148)[[146]](#cite_note-:1-149) As of 2018, further [civil war in Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopian_civil_conflict_(2018%E2%80%93present) "Ethiopian civil conflict (2018–present)") continues, mainly due to destabilization of the country.

[Ethnic violence rose](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Ethiopia#Ethnic_violence "Human rights in Ethiopia") during the late 2010s and early 2020s,[[147]](#cite_note-150)[[148]](#cite_note-151) with various clashes and conflicts leading to millions of Ethiopians being displaced.[[149]](#cite_note-irinnews-152)[[150]](#cite_note-153)[[151]](#cite_note-154)

The federal government decided that elections for 2020 (later being [rescheduled to 2021](/wiki/2021_Ethiopian_general_election "2021 Ethiopian general election")) be cancelled, due to health and safety concerns about [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Ethiopia "COVID-19 pandemic in Ethiopia").[[152]](#cite_note-155) The [Tigray Region](/wiki/Tigray_Region "Tigray Region")'s TPLF opposed this, and [proceeded to hold elections](/wiki/2020_Tigray_regional_election "2020 Tigray regional election") anyway on 9 September 2020.[[153]](#cite_note-156)[[154]](#cite_note-157) Relations between the federal government and Tigray deteriorated rapidly,[[155]](#cite_note-158) and in November 2020, Ethiopia began a military offensive in Tigray in response to [attacks on army units stationed](/wiki/Northern_Command_attacks_(Ethiopia) "Northern Command attacks (Ethiopia)") there, marking the beginning of the [Tigray war](/wiki/Tigray_war "Tigray war").[[156]](#cite_note-159)[[157]](#cite_note-160) By March 2022, as many as 500,000 people had died as a result of [violence](/wiki/War_crimes_in_the_Tigray_war "War crimes in the Tigray war") and [famine](/wiki/Famine_in_northern_Ethiopia_(2020%E2%80%93present) "Famine in northern Ethiopia (2020–present)").[[158]](#cite_note-161)[[159]](#cite_note-162)[[160]](#cite_note-163) After a number of [peace and mediation proposals](/wiki/Tigrayan_peace_process "Tigrayan peace process") in the intervening years, Ethiopia and the Tigrayan rebel forces agreed to a [cessation of hostilities on 2 November 2022](/wiki/Ethiopia%E2%80%93Tigray_peace_agreement "Ethiopia–Tigray peace agreement").[[161]](#cite_note-164) Coupled with [OLA insurgency](/wiki/OLA_insurgency "OLA insurgency"), the federal government relations with Fano militias, who previously allied to the government in the Tigray War, deteriorated in mid-2023, resulting in a [war in the Amhara Region](/wiki/War_in_Amhara "War in Amhara"). According to reports conducted by the [Ethiopian Human Rights Commission](/wiki/Ethiopian_Human_Rights_Commission "Ethiopian Human Rights Commission") (EHRC), mass human rights violations carried out by ENDF troops including door-to-door searches, extrajudicial killings, massacres and detentions. Notable incident includes the [Merawi massacre](/wiki/Merawi_massacre "Merawi massacre") in early 2024, which left 50 to 100 residents dead in [Merawi](/wiki/Merawi,_Ethiopia "Merawi, Ethiopia") town in Amhara.[[162]](#cite_note-165)[[163]](#cite_note-166)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Ethiopia](/wiki/Geography_of_Ethiopia "Geography of Ethiopia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Ethiopia_relief_location_map.jpg/250px-Ethiopia_relief_location_map.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ethiopia_relief_location_map.jpg)

Relief map of Ethiopia

At 1,104,300 square kilometres (426,372.61 sq mi),[[164]](#cite_note-CIA_World_Factbook-167) Ethiopia is the world's 26th-largest country, comparable in size to [Bolivia](/wiki/Bolivia "Bolivia"). It lies between the [3rd parallel north](/wiki/3rd_parallel_north "3rd parallel north") and the [15th parallel north](/wiki/15th_parallel_north "15th parallel north") and longitudes [33rd meridian east](/wiki/33rd_meridian_east "33rd meridian east") and [48th meridian east](/wiki/48th_meridian_east "48th meridian east").

The major portion of Ethiopia lies in the [Horn of Africa](/wiki/Horn_of_Africa "Horn of Africa"), which is the easternmost part of the African landmass. The territories that have frontiers with Ethiopia are Eritrea to the north and then, moving in a clockwise direction, Djibouti, Somalia, Kenya, South Sudan and Sudan. Within Ethiopia is a vast highland complex of mountains and dissected plateaus divided by the [Great Rift Valley](/wiki/Great_Rift_Valley,_Ethiopia "Great Rift Valley, Ethiopia"), which runs generally southwest to northeast and is surrounded by lowlands, [steppes](/wiki/Steppe "Steppe"), or semi-desert. There is a great diversity of terrain with wide variations in climate, soils, natural vegetation and settlement patterns.

Ethiopia is an ecologically diverse country, ranging from the deserts along the eastern border to the tropical forests in the south to extensive [Afromontane](/wiki/Afromontane "Afromontane") in the northern and southwestern parts. [Lake Tana](/wiki/Lake_Tana "Lake Tana") in the north is the source of the [Blue Nile](/wiki/Blue_Nile "Blue Nile"). It also has many [endemic](/wiki/Endemism "Endemism") species, notably the [gelada](/wiki/Gelada "Gelada"), the [walia ibex](/wiki/Walia_ibex "Walia ibex") and the [Ethiopian wolf](/wiki/Ethiopian_wolf "Ethiopian wolf") ("Simien fox"). The wide range of altitude has given the country a variety of ecologically distinct areas, and this has helped to encourage the evolution of endemic species in ecological isolation.

The nation is a land of geographical contrasts, ranging from the vast fertile west, with its forests and numerous rivers, to the world's hottest settlement of [Dallol](/wiki/Dallol,_Ethiopia "Dallol, Ethiopia") in its north. The [Ethiopian Highlands](/wiki/Ethiopian_Highlands "Ethiopian Highlands") are the largest continuous mountain ranges in Africa, and the [Sof Omar Caves](/wiki/Sof_Omar_Caves "Sof Omar Caves") contains the largest cave on the continent. Ethiopia also has the second-largest number of [UNESCO World Heritage Sites](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Africa "List of World Heritage Sites in Africa") in Africa.[[165]](#cite_note-168)

### Climate

Main articles: [Climate of Ethiopia](/wiki/Climate_of_Ethiopia "Climate of Ethiopia") and [Climate change in Ethiopia](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Ethiopia "Climate change in Ethiopia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_types_of_Ethiopia.svg/250px-K%C3%B6ppen_climate_types_of_Ethiopia.svg.png)](/wiki/File:K%C3%B6ppen_climate_types_of_Ethiopia.svg)

Köppen climate classification of Ethiopia

The predominant climate type is tropical monsoon, with wide topographic-induced variation. The [Ethiopian Highlands](/wiki/Ethiopian_Highlands "Ethiopian Highlands") cover most of the country and have a climate which is generally considerably cooler than other regions at similar proximity to the Equator. Most of the country's major cities are located at elevations of around 2,000–2,500 m (6,562–8,202 ft) above sea level, including historic capitals such as Gondar and Axum. The modern capital, Addis Ababa, is situated on the foothills of [Mount Entoto](/wiki/Mount_Entoto "Mount Entoto") at an elevation of around 2,400 metres (7,900 ft). It experiences a mild climate year round. With temperatures fairly uniform year round, the seasons in Addis Ababa are largely defined by rainfall: a dry season from October to February, a light rainy season from March to May, and a heavy rainy season from June to September. The average annual rainfall is approximately 1,200 millimetres (47 in).

There are on average seven hours of sunshine per day. The dry season is the sunniest time of the year, though even at the height of the rainy season in July and August there are still usually several hours per day of bright sunshine. The average annual temperature in Addis Ababa is 16 °C (60.8 °F), with daily maximum temperatures averaging 20–25 °C (68.0–77.0 °F) throughout the year, and overnight lows averaging 5–10 °C (41.0–50.0 °F).

Most major cities and tourist sites in Ethiopia lie at a similar elevation to Addis Ababa and have a comparable climate. In less elevated regions, particularly the lower lying [Ethiopian xeric grasslands and shrublands](/wiki/Ethiopian_xeric_grasslands_and_shrublands "Ethiopian xeric grasslands and shrublands") in the east of Ethiopia, the climate can be significantly hotter and drier. [Dallol](/wiki/Dallol,_Ethiopia "Dallol, Ethiopia"), in the [Danakil Depression](/wiki/Danakil_Depression "Danakil Depression") in this eastern zone, has the world's highest average annual temperature of 34 °C (93.2 °F).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Blue_Nile_Falls%2C_Ethiopia_%2851625800835%29.jpg/250px-Blue_Nile_Falls%2C_Ethiopia_%2851625800835%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Blue_Nile_Falls,_Ethiopia_(51625800835).jpg)

The [Blue Nile falls](/wiki/Blue_Nile_Falls "Blue Nile Falls") during winter provides over 86 percent of the Nile river's water coming from melted snow in the [Simien mountains](/wiki/Simien_Mountains_National_Park "Simien Mountains National Park") below 0.[[166]](#cite_note-169)[[167]](#cite_note-170)

Ethiopia is vulnerable to many of the [effects of climate change](/wiki/Effects_of_climate_change "Effects of climate change"). These include increases in temperature and changes in precipitation.[[168]](#cite_note-Misiani2020-171) [Climate change in Ethiopia](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Ethiopia "Climate change in Ethiopia") threatens food security and the economy, which is agriculture based.[[169]](#cite_note-172) Many Ethiopians have been forced to leave their homes and travel as far as the Gulf, Southern Africa and Europe.[[170]](#cite_note-173)

Since April 2019, the Ethiopian Prime Minister Abiy Ahmed has promoted [Beautifying Sheger](/wiki/Beautifying_Sheger "Beautifying Sheger"), a development project that aims to reduce the negative effects of climate change – among other things – in the capital city Addis Ababa.[[171]](#cite_note-174) In the following May, the government held "Dine for Sheger", a fundraising event in order to cover some of the $1 billion needed through the public.[[172]](#cite_note-175) $25 million was raised through the expensive event, both through the cost of attending and donations.[[173]](#cite_note-176) Two Chinese railway companies under the [Belt and Road Initiative](/wiki/Belt_and_Road_Initiative "Belt and Road Initiative") between China and Ethiopia had supplied funds to develop 12 of the total 56 kilometres.[[174]](#cite_note-177)

### Biodiversity

Main article: [Wildlife of Ethiopia](/wiki/Wildlife_of_Ethiopia "Wildlife of Ethiopia")

See also: [Environmental issues in Ethiopia](/wiki/Environmental_issues_in_Ethiopia "Environmental issues in Ethiopia") and [Deforestation in Ethiopia](/wiki/Deforestation_in_Ethiopia "Deforestation in Ethiopia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Capra_walie_87176348.jpg/250px-Capra_walie_87176348.jpg)](/wiki/File:Capra_walie_87176348.jpg)

A [Walia Ibex](/wiki/Walia_ibex "Walia ibex") in [Simien Mountains National Park](/wiki/Simien_Mountains_National_Park "Simien Mountains National Park"), one of the national symbols of Ethiopia, found only in the north of the country

Ethiopia is a global centre of avian diversity. To date more than 856 bird species have been recorded in Ethiopia, twenty of which are endemic to the country.[[175]](#cite_note-178) Sixteen species are endangered or critically endangered. Many of these birds feed on butterflies, like the *[Bicyclus anynana](/wiki/Bicyclus_anynana "Bicyclus anynana")*.[[176]](#cite_note-179)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

Historically, throughout the African continent, wildlife populations have been rapidly declining due to logging, civil wars, pollution, poaching, and other human factors.[[177]](#cite_note-180) A 17-year-long civil war, along with severe drought, negatively affected Ethiopia's environmental conditions, leading to even greater habitat degradation.[[178]](#cite_note-181) Habitat destruction is a factor that leads to endangerment. When changes to a habitat occur rapidly, animals do not have time to adjust. Human impact threatens many species, with greater threats expected as a result of climate change induced by [greenhouse gases](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas").[[179]](#cite_note-182) With carbon dioxide emissions in 2010 of 6,494,000 tonnes, Ethiopia contributes just 0.02% to the annual human-caused release of greenhouse gases.[[180]](#cite_note-183)

Ethiopia has 31 [endemic](/wiki/Endemic "Endemic") species of mammals.[[181]](#cite_note-Massicot-184) Ethiopia has many species listed as critically endangered and vulnerable to global extinction. The threatened species in Ethiopia can be broken down into three categories (based on [IUCN](/wiki/IUCN "IUCN") ratings): [critically endangered](/wiki/Critically_endangered "Critically endangered"), [endangered](/wiki/Endangered_species "Endangered species"), and [vulnerable](/wiki/Vulnerable_species "Vulnerable species").[[181]](#cite_note-Massicot-184)

Ethiopia is one of the eight fundamental and independent [centres of origin](/wiki/Centres_of_origin "Centres of origin") for cultivated plants in the world.[[182]](#cite_note-185) However, [deforestation](/wiki/Deforestation "Deforestation") is a major concern for Ethiopia as studies suggest loss of forest contributes to soil erosion, loss of nutrients in the soil, loss of animal habitats, and reduction in biodiversity. At the beginning of the 20th century, around 420,000 km2 (or 35%) of Ethiopia's land was covered by trees, but recent research indicates that forest cover is now approximately 11.9% of the area.[[183]](#cite_note-186) The country had a 2018 [Forest Landscape Integrity Index](/wiki/Forest_Landscape_Integrity_Index "Forest Landscape Integrity Index") mean score of 7.16/10, ranking it 50th globally out of 172 countries.[[184]](#cite_note-FLII-Supplementary-187)

Ethiopia loses an estimated 1,410 km2 of natural forests each year due to firewood collection, conversion to farmland, overgrazing, and use of forest wood for building material. Between 1990 and 2005 the country lost approximately 21,000 km2 of forests.[[185]](#cite_note-188) Current government programs to control deforestation consist of education, promoting reforestation programs, and providing raw materials which are alternatives to timber. In rural areas the government also provides non-timber fuel sources and access to non-forested land to promote agriculture without destroying forest habitat.[[186]](#cite_note-189)

Organizations such as SOS and Farm Africa are working with the federal government and local governments to create a system of forest management.[[187]](#cite_note-190)

## Government and politics

### Government

Main articles: [Government of Ethiopia](/wiki/Government_of_Ethiopia "Government of Ethiopia"), [Law of Ethiopia](/wiki/Law_of_Ethiopia "Law of Ethiopia"), and [Judiciary of Ethiopia](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Ethiopia "Judiciary of Ethiopia")

See also: [Rulers and Heads of State of Ethiopia](/wiki/Rulers_and_Heads_of_State_of_Ethiopia "Rulers and Heads of State of Ethiopia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Ethiopian_Parliament.jpg/250px-Ethiopian_Parliament.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ethiopian_Parliament.jpg)

[House of Peoples' Representatives](/wiki/House_of_Peoples%27_Representatives "House of Peoples' Representatives") is the lower house of the Ethiopian [Federal Parliamentary Assembly](/wiki/Federal_Parliamentary_Assembly "Federal Parliamentary Assembly").

Ethiopia is a [federal](/wiki/Federalism "Federalism") [parliamentary republic](/wiki/Parliamentary_republic "Parliamentary republic"), wherein the [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Ethiopia "Prime Minister of Ethiopia") is the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"), and the [President](/wiki/President_of_Ethiopia "President of Ethiopia") is the [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") but with largely ceremonial powers. [Executive power](/wiki/Executive_power "Executive power") is exercised by the government and federal [legislative power](/wiki/Legislative_power "Legislative power") vested in both the government and the two chambers of parliament. The [House of Federation](/wiki/House_of_Federation "House of Federation") is the upper chamber of the bicameral legislature with 108 seats, and the lower chamber is the [House of Peoples' Representatives](/wiki/House_of_Peoples%27_Representatives "House of Peoples' Representatives") (HoPR) with 547 seats. The House of Federation is chosen by the regional councils whereas MPs of the HoPR are elected directly, in turn, they elect the president for a six-year term and the prime minister for a 5-year term.[[188]](#cite_note-191)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/The_Federal_Supreme_Court_of_Ethiopia.jpg/250px-The_Federal_Supreme_Court_of_Ethiopia.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Federal_Supreme_Court_of_Ethiopia.jpg)

The [Federal Supreme Court](/wiki/Federal_Supreme_Court_of_Ethiopia "Federal Supreme Court of Ethiopia") is the highest court determining constitutionality of ordeals in the nation.

The Ethiopian [judiciary](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Ethiopia "Judiciary of Ethiopia") consists of dual system with two court structures: the federal and state courts. The FDRE Constitution vested federal judicial authority to the [Federal Supreme Court](/wiki/Federal_Supreme_Court_of_Ethiopia "Federal Supreme Court of Ethiopia") which can overturn and review decisions of subordinate federal courts; itself has regular division assigned for fundamental [errors of law](/wiki/Error_(law) "Error (law)"). In addition, the Supreme Court can perform [circuit hearings](/wiki/Circuit_court "Circuit court") in established five states at any states of federal levels or "area designated for its jurisdiction" if deemed "necessary for the efficient rendering of justice".[[189]](#cite_note-192)[[190]](#cite_note-193)

The Federal Supreme Proclamation granted three subject matter principles: laws, parties and place to federal court jurisdiction, first "cases arising under the [Constitution](/wiki/1995_Constitution_of_Ethiopia "1995 Constitution of Ethiopia"), [federal laws](/wiki/Federal_law "Federal law") and [international treaties](/wiki/International_treaties "International treaties")", second over "parties specified by federal laws".[[191]](#cite_note-194)

On the basis of Article 78 of the 1994 Ethiopian Constitution, the judiciary is completely independent of the executive and the legislature.[[192]](#cite_note-195) To ensure this, the President and Vice President of the Supreme Court are appointed by Parliament on the nomination of Prime Minister. Once elected, the executive power has no authority to remove them from office. Other judges are nominated by the [Federal Judicial Administration Council](/wiki/Federal_Judicial_Administration_Council "Federal Judicial Administration Council") (FJAC) on the basis of transparent criteria and the Prime Minister's recommendation for appointment in the HoPR. In all cases, judges cannot be removed from their duty unless they retired, violated disciplinary rules, gross incompatibility, or inefficiency to unfit due to ill health. Contrary, the majority vote of HoPR have the right to sanction removal in federal judiciary level or state council in cases of state judges.[[193]](#cite_note-:7-196) In 2015, the realities of this provision were questioned in a report prepared by [Freedom House](/wiki/Freedom_House "Freedom House").[[194]](#cite_note-197)

### Politics

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  |  |
| [Taye Atske Selassie](/wiki/Taye_Atske_Selassie "Taye Atske Selassie") [President](/wiki/President_of_Ethiopia "President of Ethiopia") (representative head of state) | [Abiy Ahmed](/wiki/Abiy_Ahmed "Abiy Ahmed") [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Ethiopia "Prime Minister of Ethiopia") (head of government) |

Post-1995, Ethiopia's politics has been liberalized which promotes all-encompassing reforms to the country. Today, its economy is based on [mixed](/wiki/Mixed_economy "Mixed economy"), [market-oriented](/wiki/Market_economy "Market economy") principles.[[193]](#cite_note-:7-196) Ethiopia has twelve semi-autonomous administrative regions that have the power to raise and spend their own revenues.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [first multiparty election](/wiki/1995_Ethiopian_general_election "1995 Ethiopian general election") took place in May 1995, which was won by the EPRDF.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELyons1996142-198) The president of the transitional government, EPRDF leader [Meles Zenawi](/wiki/Meles_Zenawi "Meles Zenawi"), became the first [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Ethiopia "Prime Minister of Ethiopia") of the Federal Democratic Republic of Ethiopia, and [Negasso Gidada](/wiki/Negasso_Gidada "Negasso Gidada") was elected its president.[[196]](#cite_note-199) Meles' government was consistently re-elected; however, these results were heavily criticized by international observers, and denounced by the opposition as fraudulent.[[197]](#cite_note-FH_Ethiopia-200)

Meles died on 20 August 2012 in Brussels, where he was being treated for an unspecified illness.[[198]](#cite_note-201) Deputy Prime Minister [Hailemariam Desalegn](/wiki/Hailemariam_Desalegn "Hailemariam Desalegn") was appointed as a new prime minister until the [2015 elections](/wiki/Ethiopian_general_election,_2015 "Ethiopian general election, 2015"),[[199]](#cite_note-202) and remained so afterwards with his party in control of every parliamentary seat.[[200]](#cite_note-AJE_HD_holds-203) On 15 February 2018, Hailemariam [resigned](/wiki/Resignation_of_Hailemariam_Desalegn "Resignation of Hailemariam Desalegn") as Prime Minister, following [years of protests](/wiki/2014%E2%80%932016_Oromo_protests "2014–2016 Oromo protests") and a [state of emergency](/wiki/2016%E2%80%932018_Ethiopian_state_of_emergency "2016–2018 Ethiopian state of emergency").[[201]](#cite_note-Reuters1-204)[[202]](#cite_note-205)[[203]](#cite_note-206) [Abiy Ahmed](/wiki/Abiy_Ahmed "Abiy Ahmed") became prime minister following Hailemariam's resignation. He made a historic visit to Eritrea in 2018, ending the state of conflict between the two countries,[[146]](#cite_note-:1-149) and was awarded the [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize") in 2019.[[204]](#cite_note-207)

According to the [Democracy Index](/wiki/Democracy_Index "Democracy Index") published by the United Kingdom-based [Economist Intelligence Unit](/wiki/Economist_Intelligence_Unit "Economist Intelligence Unit") in late 2010, Ethiopia was an "authoritarian regime", ranking as the 118th-most democratic out of 167 countries.[[205]](#cite_note-208) Ethiopia had dropped 13 places on the list since 2008, and the 2010 report attributed the drop to the government's crackdown on opposition activities, media, and civil society before the [2010 parliamentary election](/wiki/Ethiopian_general_election,_2010 "Ethiopian general election, 2010"), which the report argued had made Ethiopia a [*de facto* one-party state](/wiki/Dominant-party_system "Dominant-party system").[[206]](#cite_note-209)

Accompanied by pervasive [internal and intercommunal conflicts in the 21st century](/wiki/Ethnic_discrimination_in_Ethiopia "Ethnic discrimination in Ethiopia"), the Ethiopian government resorted to authoritarian structure, severing democratic and human rights.[[207]](#cite_note-210) [Freedom House](/wiki/Freedom_House "Freedom House"), who has worked on Ethiopia since 2008, indicates that Ethiopia is "Not Free" state due to very poor [fundamental rights](/wiki/Fundamental_rights "Fundamental rights") (political and civil liberties) recorded in both [EPRDF](/wiki/Ethiopian_People%27s_Revolutionary_Democratic_Front "Ethiopian People's Revolutionary Democratic Front") and [Prosperity Party](/wiki/Prosperity_Party "Prosperity Party") regimes.[[208]](#cite_note-211)[[209]](#cite_note-212) Under Abiy Ahmed, Ethiopia is experiencing [democratic backsliding](/wiki/Democratic_backsliding_in_Ethiopia "Democratic backsliding in Ethiopia") since 2019 marked by turbulent period of [internal conflict](/wiki/Ethiopian_civil_conflict_(2018%E2%80%93present) "Ethiopian civil conflict (2018–present)"), jailing opposition group members and limiting media freedom.[[210]](#cite_note-213)[[211]](#cite_note-214)[[212]](#cite_note-215)

### Administrative divisions

Main articles: [Subdivisions of Ethiopia](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_Ethiopia "Subdivisions of Ethiopia"), [Regions of Ethiopia](/wiki/Regions_of_Ethiopia "Regions of Ethiopia"), [List of zones of Ethiopia](/wiki/List_of_zones_of_Ethiopia "List of zones of Ethiopia"), [Districts of Ethiopia](/wiki/Districts_of_Ethiopia "Districts of Ethiopia"), and [Wards of Ethiopia](/wiki/Wards_of_Ethiopia "Wards of Ethiopia")

Ethiopia is [administratively divided](/wiki/Administrative_division "Administrative division") into four levels: [regions](/wiki/Regions_of_Ethiopia "Regions of Ethiopia"), [zones](/wiki/List_of_zones_of_Ethiopia "List of zones of Ethiopia"), [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Ethiopia "Districts of Ethiopia") (*woredas*) and [wards](/wiki/Wards_of_Ethiopia "Wards of Ethiopia") (*kebele*).[[213]](#cite_note-mula-216)[[214]](#cite_note-mappr-217) The country comprises 12 regions and two city administrations under these regions, plenty of zones, woredas and neighbourhood administration: kebeles. The two federal-level city administrations are Addis Ababa and Dire Dawa.[[215]](#cite_note-Comparison-218)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Map_of_zones_of_Ethiopia.svg/960px-Map_of_zones_of_Ethiopia.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_zones_of_Ethiopia.svg)

Map of [regions](/wiki/Regions_of_Ethiopia "Regions of Ethiopia") and [zones](/wiki/List_of_zones_of_Ethiopia "List of zones of Ethiopia") of Ethiopia

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Ethiopia](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Ethiopia "Foreign relations of Ethiopia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/A_Group_photo_of_Leaders_of_G-8%2C_Outreach_five_countries_and_Africa_Outreach_countries_at_G_8_Summit_in_Heiligendamm%2C_Germany_on_June_08%2C_2007.jpg/250px-A_Group_photo_of_Leaders_of_G-8%2C_Outreach_five_countries_and_Africa_Outreach_countries_at_G_8_Summit_in_Heiligendamm%2C_Germany_on_June_08%2C_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_Group_photo_of_Leaders_of_G-8,_Outreach_five_countries_and_Africa_Outreach_countries_at_G_8_Summit_in_Heiligendamm,_Germany_on_June_08,_2007.jpg)

Ethiopian [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Ethiopia "Prime Minister of Ethiopia") [Meles Zenawi](/wiki/Meles_Zenawi "Meles Zenawi") (bottom row, fourth from left) in G8 Group meeting in 2007

Ethiopia was historically a [trading nation](/wiki/Trading_nation "Trading nation") that exported goods such as [gold](/wiki/Gold "Gold"), [ivory](/wiki/Ivory "Ivory"), exotic animals, and [incense](/wiki/Incense "Incense").[[216]](#cite_note-219) Modern Ethiopian foreign relations began under [Emperor Tewodros II](/wiki/Tewodros_II "Tewodros II"), who during his reign sought to re-establish a cohesive Ethiopian state, but was thwarted by the [British expedition of 1868](/wiki/1868_Expedition_to_Abyssinia "1868 Expedition to Abyssinia").[[217]](#cite_note-220) Since then, the country was seen as redundant by world powers until the opening of [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez_Canal "Suez Canal") due to an influence of [Mahdist War](/wiki/Mahdist_War "Mahdist War").[[218]](#cite_note-221)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/African_Union_Headquarters_Addis_Ababa.jpg/250px-African_Union_Headquarters_Addis_Ababa.jpg)](/wiki/File:African_Union_Headquarters_Addis_Ababa.jpg)

The [African Union Headquarters](/wiki/African_Union_Conference_Center_and_Office_Complex "African Union Conference Center and Office Complex") located in [Addis Ababa](/wiki/Addis_Ababa "Addis Ababa"). Since its conception, Ethiopia is a founding member to the AU.

Today, Ethiopia maintains strong relations with [China](/wiki/China%E2%80%93Ethiopia_relations "China–Ethiopia relations"), [Israel](/wiki/Ethiopia%E2%80%93Israel_relations "Ethiopia–Israel relations"), [Mexico](/wiki/Ethiopia%E2%80%93Mexico_relations "Ethiopia–Mexico relations"), [Turkey](/wiki/Ethiopia%E2%80%93Turkey_relations "Ethiopia–Turkey relations") and [India](/wiki/Ethiopia%E2%80%93India_relations "Ethiopia–India relations") as well as neighboring countries. Ethiopia is a strategic partner of Global [War on Terrorism](/wiki/War_on_Terrorism "War on Terrorism") and [African Growth and Opportunity Act](/wiki/African_Growth_and_Opportunity_Act "African Growth and Opportunity Act") (AGOA).[[219]](#cite_note-222) US.
