Former [U.S. President](/wiki/U.S._President "U.S. President") [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") was the first incumbent U.S. president to visit Ethiopia in July 2015; the speech he gave to the [African Union](/wiki/African_Union "African Union") during this trip focused on combatting Islamic terrorism.[[220]](#cite_note-223)[[221]](#cite_note-224) [Emigration from Ethiopia](/wiki/Emigration_from_Ethiopia "Emigration from Ethiopia") is primarily directed towards Europe, including [Italy](/wiki/Ethiopians_in_Italy "Ethiopians in Italy"), the [United Kingdom](/wiki/Ethiopians_in_the_United_Kingdom "Ethiopians in the United Kingdom") and [Sweden](/wiki/Ethiopians_in_Sweden "Ethiopians in Sweden"), as well as [Canada](/wiki/Ethiopian_Canadians "Ethiopian Canadians") and [Australia](/wiki/Ethiopian_Australians "Ethiopian Australians"), while emigration to the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East") is primarily to [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") and [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"). Ethiopia is founding member of the [Group of 24](/wiki/Group_of_24 "Group of 24") (G-24), the [Non-Aligned Movement](/wiki/Non-Aligned_Movement "Non-Aligned Movement") and the [G77](/wiki/Group_of_77 "Group of 77"). In 1963, the [Organization of African Unity](/wiki/Organization_of_African_Unity "Organization of African Unity"), which later renamed itself the African Union, was founded in Addis Ababa, which today hosts the secretariat of the African Union, the [African Union Commission](/wiki/African_Union_Commission "African Union Commission"). In addition, Ethiopia is also a member of the [Pan African Chamber of Commerce and Industry](/wiki/Pan_African_Chamber_of_Commerce_and_Industry "Pan African Chamber of Commerce and Industry"), the [United Nations Economic Commission for Africa](/wiki/United_Nations_Economic_Commission_for_Africa "United Nations Economic Commission for Africa"), the [African Standby Force](/wiki/African_Standby_Force "African Standby Force")[[222]](#cite_note-225) and many of global [NGOs](/wiki/NGO "NGO") focused on Africa.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Abiy_Ahmed_with_Vladimir_Putin.jpg/250px-Abiy_Ahmed_with_Vladimir_Putin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abiy_Ahmed_with_Vladimir_Putin.jpg)

Prime Minister [Abiy Ahmed](/wiki/Abiy_Ahmed "Abiy Ahmed") with Russian President [Vladimir Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin") in 2023

Ethiopia's foreign relations with both [Sudan](/wiki/Ethiopia%E2%80%93Sudan_relations "Ethiopia–Sudan relations") and [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt%E2%80%93Ethiopia_relations "Egypt–Ethiopia relations") are somewhat fraught owing to the effects the [Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam](/wiki/Grand_Ethiopian_Renaissance_Dam "Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam") project, which was escalated in 2020, would have on [water rights](/wiki/Water_resources_law "Water resources law") in the region.[[223]](#cite_note-226)[[224]](#cite_note-227) Despite six upstream countries (Ethiopia, [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya"), [Uganda](/wiki/Uganda "Uganda"), [Rwanda](/wiki/Rwanda "Rwanda"), [Burundi](/wiki/Burundi "Burundi"), and [Tanzania](/wiki/Tanzania "Tanzania")) signing the Nile Basin Initiative in 2010, Egypt and Sudan rejected a water sharing treaty, citing the reduction of amount of water to the [Nile Basin](/wiki/Nile_Basin "Nile Basin") and the challenge it would pose to their historic connection of [water rights](/wiki/Water_rights "Water rights").[[225]](#cite_note-228)[[226]](#cite_note-229) In 2020, Prime Minister Abiy Ahmed warned that "No force can stop Ethiopia from building a dam. If there is need to go to war, we could get millions readied."[[227]](#cite_note-230)

Ethiopia is one of the African countries that was a founding member of [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations"), which served as the predecessor for the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), since 1923. UN taskforces in Ethiopia deal primarily with humanitarian issues and development. Some of its agencies[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*] maintain regional ties with [United Nations Economic Commission for Africa](/wiki/United_Nations_Economic_Commission_for_Africa "United Nations Economic Commission for Africa") and the African Union. The UN prioritizes [sustainable development](/wiki/Sustainable_development "Sustainable development") in Ethiopia, including fighting poverty, sustainable economic growth, [climate change policy](/wiki/Climate_change_policy "Climate change policy"), educational and healthcare provisions, increasing employment, and environmental protection.[[228]](#cite_note-231)

### Military

Main articles: [Ethiopian National Defense Force](/wiki/Ethiopian_National_Defense_Force "Ethiopian National Defense Force") and [Military history of Ethiopia](/wiki/Military_history_of_Ethiopia "Military history of Ethiopia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Ethiopian_military_serving_under_the_African_Union_Mission_in_Somalia_in_sector_4_based_at_Beletweyne_carried_out_live-fire_drills.jpg/250px-Ethiopian_military_serving_under_the_African_Union_Mission_in_Somalia_in_sector_4_based_at_Beletweyne_carried_out_live-fire_drills.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ethiopian_military_serving_under_the_African_Union_Mission_in_Somalia_in_sector_4_based_at_Beletweyne_carried_out_live-fire_drills.jpg)

Ethiopian National Defense Force (ENDF) training under [AMISOM](/wiki/African_Union_Mission_to_Somalia "African Union Mission to Somalia"), 2021

The Ethiopian army's origins and military traditions date back to the earliest [history of Ethiopia](/wiki/History_of_Ethiopia "History of Ethiopia"). Due to Ethiopia's location between the Middle East and Africa, it has long been in the middle of Eastern and Western politics and has been subject to foreign invasions. In 1579, the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire")'s attempt to expand from a coastal base at [Massawa](/wiki/Massawa "Massawa") during the [Ottoman conquest of Habesh](/wiki/Ottoman_conquest_of_Habesh "Ottoman conquest of Habesh") was defeated.[[229]](#cite_note-232) The [Army of the Ethiopian Empire](/wiki/Army_of_the_Ethiopian_Empire "Army of the Ethiopian Empire") was also able to defeat the Egyptians in 1876 at [Gura](/wiki/Battle_of_Gura "Battle of Gura"), led by Ethiopian Emperor [Yohannes IV](/wiki/Yohannes_IV "Yohannes IV").[[230]](#cite_note-233) Ethiopia only has 3 branches inside the military, consisting of the [Army](/wiki/Ethiopian_Ground_Forces "Ethiopian Ground Forces"), [Airforce](/wiki/Ethiopian_Air_Force "Ethiopian Air Force"), and a [Navy](/wiki/Ethiopian_Navy "Ethiopian Navy") although it is landlocked. With an annual budget of over $1 billion dollars, it is the largest armed force in East Africa, and one of the largest in Africa.[[231]](#cite_note-234)[[232]](#cite_note-235)

### Law enforcement

Ethiopia has two main federal law enforcement agencies at the federal level, to which being the [Ethiopian Federal Police](/wiki/Ethiopian_Federal_Police "Ethiopian Federal Police"), and the [National Intelligence Security Service](/wiki/National_Intelligence_and_Security_Service_(Ethiopia) "National Intelligence and Security Service (Ethiopia)"). The [NISS](/wiki/National_Intelligence_and_Security_Service_(Ethiopia) "National Intelligence and Security Service (Ethiopia)") serving both as a spy agency and domestic law enforcement agency has national jurisdiction for counterterrorism, and foreign related counter espionage activities and protecting [national security](/wiki/National_security "National security"). It also has authority related to international economic crimes related of which to Ethiopia as a whole.[[233]](#cite_note-236)[[234]](#cite_note-237) It alongside the [EFP](/wiki/Ethiopian_Federal_Police "Ethiopian Federal Police") (Ethiopian Federal Police) who both enforce federal laws domestically and for the [National Intelligence Security Service](/wiki/National_Intelligence_and_Security_Service_(Ethiopia) "National Intelligence and Security Service (Ethiopia)") cooperate internationally and enforce border regulations and peoples and products coming in and out in Ethiopia.[[235]](#cite_note-:0-238) The EFP mostly enforcing non-international, and non-espionage crimes, enforcing [civil rights](/wiki/Civil_and_political_rights "Civil and political rights") related subjects of matter and domestic financial crimes. At the regional level Ethiopia has 12 regionals statewide police departments, and 2 federally chartered police forces, those being the Addis Ababa Police, and Dire Dawa Police forces.[[236]](#cite_note-:32-239)[[237]](#cite_note-:13-240)

## Economy

Main articles: [Economy of Ethiopia](/wiki/Economy_of_Ethiopia "Economy of Ethiopia") and [Industrial sector in Ethiopia](/wiki/Industrial_sector_in_Ethiopia "Industrial sector in Ethiopia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/GDP_per_capita_development_of_Ethiopia.svg/250px-GDP_per_capita_development_of_Ethiopia.svg.png)](/wiki/File:GDP_per_capita_development_of_Ethiopia.svg)

Development of GDP per capita

Ethiopia registered the fastest economic growth under [Meles Zenawi's administration](/wiki/Premiership_of_Meles_Zenawi "Premiership of Meles Zenawi").[[238]](#cite_note-241)
According to the [IMF](/wiki/IMF "IMF"), Ethiopia was one of the fastest growing economies in the world, registering over 10% economic growth from 2004 through 2009.[[239]](#cite_note-Outlook_2012-242) It was the fastest-growing non-oil-dependent African economy in the years 2007 and 2008.[[240]](#cite_note-243) In 2015, the World Bank highlighted that Ethiopia had witnessed rapid economic growth with real domestic product (GDP) growth averaging 10.9% between 2004 and 2014.[[241]](#cite_note-244)

In 2008 and 2011, Ethiopia's growth performance and considerable development gains were challenged by high [inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation") and a difficult [balance of payments](/wiki/Balance_of_payments "Balance of payments") situation. Inflation surged to 40% in August 2011 because of loose [monetary policy](/wiki/Monetary_policy "Monetary policy"), large [civil service](/wiki/Civil_service "Civil service") wage increase in early 2011, and high food prices.[[242]](#cite_note-Ethiopia_Overview-245)

In spite of fast growth in recent years, GDP per capita is one of the lowest in the world, and the economy faces a number of serious structural problems. Based on the most recent data from 2019, 68.7% of the population is affected by [multidimensional poverty](/wiki/Multidimensional_Poverty_Index "Multidimensional Poverty Index") and an additional 18.4% vulnerable to it.[[34]](#cite_note-:2-36)

With a focused investment in public infrastructure and industrial parks, Ethiopia is attempting to address its structural problems by becoming a hub for light manufacturing in Africa.[[243]](#cite_note-246) In 2019 a law was passed allowing expatriate Ethiopians to invest in Ethiopia's financial service industry.[[244]](#cite_note-247)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Ethiopian_cargo_ship_at_Port_of_Djibouti.jpg/250px-Ethiopian_cargo_ship_at_Port_of_Djibouti.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ethiopian_cargo_ship_at_Port_of_Djibouti.jpg)

An Ethiopian logistics shipping cargo docked at the Red Sea

The Ethiopian constitution specifies that rights to own land belong only to "the state and the people", but citizens may lease land for up to 99 years, but are unable to mortgage or sell. Renting out land for a maximum of twenty years is allowed and this is expected to ensure that land goes to the most productive user.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Land distribution and administration is considered an area where corruption is institutionalized, and facilitation payments as well as bribes are often demanded when dealing with land-related issues.[[245]](#cite_note-248) As there is no land ownership, infrastructural projects are most often simply done without asking the land users, which then end up being displaced and without a home or land. A lot of anger and distrust sometimes results in public protests. In addition, agricultural productivity remains low, and frequent droughts still beset the country, also leading to internal displacement.[[246]](#cite_note-249)

### Energy and hydropower

See also: [Energy in Ethiopia](/wiki/Energy_in_Ethiopia "Energy in Ethiopia") and [List of power stations in Ethiopia](/wiki/List_of_power_stations_in_Ethiopia "List of power stations in Ethiopia")

Ethiopia has 14 major rivers flowing from its highlands, including the Nile. It has the largest water reserves in Africa. As of 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ethiopia&action=edit), hydroelectric plants represented around 88.2% of the total installed electricity generating capacity.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/GERD_3.jpg/330px-GERD_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:GERD_3.jpg)

The [Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam](/wiki/Grand_Ethiopian_Renaissance_Dam "Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam") constructed between 2011 and 2023, set to be the largest dam in Africa

The remaining electrical power was generated from fossil fuels (8.3%) and renewable sources (3.6%).

The electrification rate for the total population in 2016 was 42%, with 85% coverage in urban areas and 26% coverage in rural areas. As of 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ethiopia&action=edit), total electricity production was 11.15 TW⋅h and consumption was 9.062 TW⋅h. There were 0.166 TW⋅h of electricity exported, 0 kW⋅h imported, and 2.784 GW of installed generating capacity.[[18]](#cite_note-CIA2-20)
Ethiopia delivers roughly 81% of water volume to the Nile through the river basins of the [Blue Nile](/wiki/Blue_Nile "Blue Nile"), [Sobat River](/wiki/Sobat_River "Sobat River") and [Atbara](/wiki/Atbara "Atbara"). In 1959, Egypt and Sudan signed a bilateral treaty, the [1959 Nile Waters Agreement](/wiki/Water_politics_in_the_Nile_Basin "Water politics in the Nile Basin"), which gave both countries exclusive maritime rights over the Nile waters. Ever since, Egypt has discouraged almost all projects in Ethiopia that sought to use the local Nile tributaries. This had the effect of discouraging external financing of hydropower and irrigation projects in western Ethiopia, thereby impeding water resource-based economic development projects. However, Ethiopia is in the process of constructing a large 6,450 MW hydroelectric dam on the Blue Nile river. Completed in 2023, this [Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam](/wiki/Grand_Ethiopian_Renaissance_Dam "Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam") is slated to be the largest hydroelectric power station in Africa.[[247]](#cite_note-250) The [Gibe III](/wiki/Gilgel_Gibe_III_Dam "Gilgel Gibe III Dam") hydroelectric project is so far the largest in the country with an installed capacity of 1,870 MW. For the year 2017–18 (2010 E.C) this hydroelectric dam generated 4,900 GW⋅h.[[248]](#cite_note-251)

### Agriculture

Main article: [Agriculture in Ethiopia](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Ethiopia "Agriculture in Ethiopia")

Agriculture constitutes around 85% of the labour force. However, the service sector represents the largest portion of the [GDP](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product").[[18]](#cite_note-CIA2-20) Many other economic activities depend on agriculture, including marketing, processing, and export of agricultural products. Production is overwhelmingly by small-scale farmers and enterprises, and a large part of commodity exports are provided by the small agricultural cash-crop sector. Principal crops include [coffee](/wiki/Coffea "Coffea"), [legumes](/wiki/Legume "Legume"), [oilseeds](/wiki/Rapeseed "Rapeseed"), [cereals](/wiki/Cereal "Cereal"), potatoes, [sugarcane](/wiki/Sugarcane "Sugarcane"), and vegetables. Ethiopia is also a [Vavilov](/wiki/Center_of_origin "Center of origin") centre of diversity for domesticated crops, including [enset](/wiki/Ensete_ventricosum "Ensete ventricosum"),[[249]](#cite_note-252) [coffee](/wiki/Coffee_bean "Coffee bean"), okra and [teff](/wiki/Teff "Teff").

Exports are almost entirely agricultural commodities (with the exception of [gold](/wiki/Gold "Gold") exports), and coffee is the largest foreign exchange earner. Ethiopia is Africa's second biggest [maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize") producer.[[250]](#cite_note-253) According to UN estimations, the per capita GDP of Ethiopia has reached $357 as of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ethiopia&action=edit).[[251]](#cite_note-auto-254)

### Exports

Main article: [Coffee production in Ethiopia](/wiki/Coffee_production_in_Ethiopia "Coffee production in Ethiopia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/ET_Addis_asv2018-01_img07_Light_Rail_%28cropped2%29.jpg/250px-ET_Addis_asv2018-01_img07_Light_Rail_%28cropped2%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:ET_Addis_asv2018-01_img07_Light_Rail_(cropped2).jpg)

The [Addis Ababa Light Rail](/wiki/Addis_Ababa_Light_Rail "Addis Ababa Light Rail") system carries over 50,000 passengers a day.[[252]](#cite_note-255)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/ET-ASK_-_Ras_Dashen_-_Ethiopian_Airlines_-_Boeing_777-360ER_-_MSN_44550_-_VGHS.jpg/250px-ET-ASK_-_Ras_Dashen_-_Ethiopian_Airlines_-_Boeing_777-360ER_-_MSN_44550_-_VGHS.jpg)](/wiki/File:ET-ASK_-_Ras_Dashen_-_Ethiopian_Airlines_-_Boeing_777-360ER_-_MSN_44550_-_VGHS.jpg)

Ethiopia's biggest company [Ethiopian Airlines](/wiki/Ethiopian_Airlines "Ethiopian Airlines"), which has an annual revenue of 6.1 billion dollars and making it the 14th biggest airlines in revenue, contributes to the country's export business.[[253]](#cite_note-256)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Ethio_Telecom_%2825070216192%29.jpg/250px-Ethio_Telecom_%2825070216192%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ethio_Telecom_(25070216192).jpg)

[Ethio Telecom](/wiki/Ethio_Telecom "Ethio Telecom") is one of the country's biggest companies which generates over a billion dollars annually.[[254]](#cite_note-257)

Ethiopia is often considered as the birthplace of [coffee](/wiki/Coffee "Coffee") since cultivation began in the 9th century.[[255]](#cite_note-:6-258) Exports from Ethiopia in the 2009–2010 financial year totalled US$1.4 billion.[[256]](#cite_note-259) Ethiopia produces more coffee than any other nation on the continent.[[257]](#cite_note-260) "Coffee provides a livelihood for close to 15 million Ethiopians, 16% of the population. Farmers in the eastern part of the country, where a warming climate is already impacting production, have struggled in recent years, and many are currently reporting largely failed harvests as a result of a prolonged drought".[[258]](#cite_note-261)

Ethiopia also has the fifth largest inventory of [cattle](/wiki/Arado_cattle "Arado cattle").[[259]](#cite_note-262) Other main export commodities are [khat](/wiki/Khat "Khat"), gold, leather products, and oilseeds. Recent development of the [floriculture](/wiki/Floriculture "Floriculture") sector means Ethiopia is poised to become one of the top flower and plant exporters in the world.[[260]](#cite_note-263)

Cross-border trade by pastoralists is often informal and beyond state control and regulation. In [East Africa](/wiki/East_Africa "East Africa"), over 95% of cross-border trade is through unofficial channels. The unofficial trade of live cattle, camels, sheep, and goats from Ethiopia sold to [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia"), [Djibouti](/wiki/Djibouti "Djibouti"), and [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya") generates an estimated total value of US$250–300 million annually (100 times more than the official figure).[[261]](#cite_note-ODI-264)

This trade helps lower food prices, increase food security, relieve border tensions, and promote regional integration.[[261]](#cite_note-ODI-264) However, the unregulated and undocumented nature of this trade runs risks, such as allowing disease to spread more easily across national borders. Furthermore, the government of Ethiopia is purportedly unhappy with lost tax revenue and foreign exchange revenues.[[261]](#cite_note-ODI-264) Recent initiatives have sought to document and regulate this trade.[[261]](#cite_note-ODI-264)

With the private sector growing slowly, designer [fashion](/wiki/Fashion_in_Ethiopia "Fashion in Ethiopia") and leather products like bags are becoming a big export business, with Taytu becoming the first luxury designer label in the country.[[262]](#cite_note-265) Additional small-scale export products include cereals, pulses, cotton, sugarcane, potatoes, and hides. With the construction of various new dams and growing hydroelectric power projects around the country, Ethiopia also plans to export electric power to its neighbours.[[263]](#cite_note-266)[[264]](#cite_note-267)

Most regard Ethiopia's large water resources and potential as its "white oil" and its coffee resources as "black gold".[[265]](#cite_note-268)[[266]](#cite_note-269)

### Transport

Main article: [Transport in Ethiopia](/wiki/Transport_in_Ethiopia "Transport in Ethiopia")

Two trans-African automobile routes pass through Ethiopia: the [Cairo-Cape Town Highway](/wiki/Cairo-Cape_Town_Highway "Cairo-Cape Town Highway") and the [N'Djamena-Djibouti Highway](/wiki/N%27Djamena-Djibouti_Highway "N'Djamena-Djibouti Highway"). Ethiopia has 926 km of electrified 1,435 mm (4 ft 8+1⁄2 in) [standard gauge](/wiki/Standard_gauge "Standard gauge") railways, 656 km for the [Addis Ababa–Djibouti Railway](/wiki/Addis_Ababa%E2%80%93Djibouti_Railway "Addis Ababa–Djibouti Railway") between Addis Ababa and the [Port of Djibouti](/wiki/Port_of_Djibouti "Port of Djibouti") (via [Awash](/wiki/Awash,_Ethiopia "Awash, Ethiopia"))[[267]](#cite_note-270) and 270 km for the [Awash–Hara Gebeya Railway](/wiki/Awash%E2%80%93Hara_Gebeya_Railway "Awash–Hara Gebeya Railway") between Addis Ababa and the [twin cities](/wiki/Twin_cities "Twin cities") of [Dessie](/wiki/Dessie "Dessie")/[Kombolcha](/wiki/Kombolcha "Kombolcha").[[268]](#cite_note-AKH-271)

Ethiopia had 58 airports as of 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ethiopia&action=edit),[[18]](#cite_note-CIA2-20) and 61 as of 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ethiopia&action=edit).[[269]](#cite_note-272) Among these, the [Bole International Airport](/wiki/Bole_International_Airport "Bole International Airport") in Addis Ababa and the [Aba Tenna Dejazmach Yilma International Airport](/wiki/Aba_Tenna_Dejazmach_Yilma_International_Airport "Aba Tenna Dejazmach Yilma International Airport") in Dire Dawa accommodate international flights.

### Science and technology

Main article: [Science and technology in Ethiopia](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Ethiopia "Science and technology in Ethiopia")

See also: [List of Ethiopian scientists](/wiki/List_of_Ethiopian_scientists "List of Ethiopian scientists")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Portrait-aklilu-lemma-11.jpg/250px-Portrait-aklilu-lemma-11.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait-aklilu-lemma-11.jpg)

Pathobiologist [Aklilu Lemma](/wiki/Aklilu_Lemma "Aklilu Lemma"). In 1964, he discovered an [alternative](/wiki/Alternative_medicine "Alternative medicine") treatment for [schistosomiasis](/wiki/Schistosomiasis "Schistosomiasis"), known as snail fever.[[270]](#cite_note-273)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Zeresenay_Alemseged_%282013-01%29.JPG/250px-Zeresenay_Alemseged_%282013-01%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Zeresenay_Alemseged_(2013-01).JPG)

Paleoanthropologist [Zeresenay Alemseged](/wiki/Zeresenay_Alemseged "Zeresenay Alemseged") in 2013. He was best known for discovering fossilized [hominin](/wiki/Hominin "Hominin") called *[Selam](/wiki/Selam_(Australopithecus) "Selam (Australopithecus)")* or "Lucy's baby" in December 2000.[[271]](#cite_note-274)

Science and technology in Ethiopia emerging as progressive due to lack of organized institutions. Manufacturing and service providers often place themselves in [competitive programming](/wiki/Competitive_programming "Competitive programming") in order to advance innovative and technological solutions through in-house arenas.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] The [Ethiopian Space Science and Technology](/wiki/Ethiopian_Space_Science_and_Technology_Institute "Ethiopian Space Science and Technology Institute") is responsible for conducting multifaceted tasks regarding space and technology. In addition, Ethiopia also launched 70 kg ET-RSS1 multi-spectral remote sensing satellite in December 2019. The President Sahle-Work Zewde told prior in October 2019 that "the satellite will provide all the necessary data on changes in climate and weather-related phenomena that would be used for the country's key targets in agriculture, forestry as well as natural resources protection initiatives." By January 2020, satellite manufacturing, assembling, integrating and testing began. This would also incremented facility built by French company funded by European Investment Bank (EIB). The main observatory Entoto Observatory and Space Science Research Center (EORC) allocated space programmes. The Ethiopian Biotechnology Institute is a part of Scientific Research & Development Services Industry, responsible for environmental and climate conservation.[[272]](#cite_note-275) Numerous profound scientists have contributed degree of honours and reputations. Some are [Kitaw Ejigu](/wiki/Kitaw_Ejigu "Kitaw Ejigu"), [Mulugeta Bekele](/wiki/Mulugeta_Bekele "Mulugeta Bekele"), [Aklilu Lemma](/wiki/Aklilu_Lemma "Aklilu Lemma"), [Gebisa Ejeta](/wiki/Gebisa_Ejeta "Gebisa Ejeta") and [Melaku Worede](/wiki/Melaku_Worede "Melaku Worede"). Computer scientist [Timnit Gebru](/wiki/Timnit_Gebru "Timnit Gebru"), named one of [Time's](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)") most influential people in 2022, was born in Ethiopia.[[273]](#cite_note-276)

Ethiopia is known for the use of [traditional medicine](/wiki/Traditional_medicine "Traditional medicine") since millennia. The first epidemic that occurred in Ethiopia was in 849, causing the Aksumite Emperor Abba Yohannes evicted from place due to "God's punishment for misdeeds". [*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] The first traditional medicinal remedy was claimed to be derived from this catastrophe, but the exact source is debated. Though it differs among ethnic groups, traditional medicine often implements herbs, spiritual healing, bone-setting and minor surgical procedures in treating disease.[[274]](#cite_note-277)

Ethiopia was ranked 134th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[275]](#cite_note-278)[[276]](#cite_note-279)

Ethiopia is the most populous landlocked country in the world.[[277]](#cite_note-ciawfb-280) Its total population has grown substantially in recent decades, rising from 38.1 million in 1983 to 135 million in 2025.[[17]](#cite_note-:10-19)[[278]](#cite_note-281) The 2050 Ethiopia population forecast is 225 million.[[279]](#cite_note-282) According to UN estimations in 2013, life expectancy had improved substantially over time, with male life expectancy reported to be 56 years and for women 60 years.[[251]](#cite_note-auto-254)

Ethiopia's population is highly diverse, containing over 80 different ethnic groups, the four largest of which are the [Oromo](/wiki/Oromo_people "Oromo people"), [Amhara](/wiki/Amhara_people "Amhara people"), [Somali](/wiki/Somalis "Somalis") and [Tigrayans](/wiki/Tigrayans "Tigrayans"). According to the Ethiopian national census of 2007, the [Oromo](/wiki/Oromo_people "Oromo people") are the largest ethnic group in Ethiopia, at 34.4% of the nation's population. The [Amhara](/wiki/Amhara_people "Amhara people") represent 27.0% of the country's inhabitants, while [Somalis](/wiki/Somalis "Somalis") and [Tigrayans](/wiki/Tigrayans "Tigrayans") represent 6.2% and 6.1% of the population respectively.[[6]](#cite_note-2007Census-7)

[Afroasiatic-speaking](/wiki/Afroasiatic_languages "Afroasiatic languages") communities make up the majority of the population. Among these, Semitic speakers often collectively refer to themselves as the [Habesha people](/wiki/Habesha_people "Habesha people"). The [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") form of this term (*al-Ḥabasha*) is the etymological basis of "Abyssinia", the former name of Ethiopia in English and other European languages.[[280]](#cite_note-283)

In 2009, Ethiopia hosted a population of refugees and asylum seekers numbering approximately 135,200. The majority of this population came from Somalia (approximately 64,300 persons), Eritrea (41,700) and Sudan (25,900). The Ethiopian government required nearly all refugees to live in refugee camps.[[281]](#cite_note-World_Refugee_Survey_2009-284)

### Urbanization

Main articles: [List of cities and towns in Ethiopia](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Ethiopia "List of cities and towns in Ethiopia") and [Urbanization in Ethiopia](/wiki/Urbanization_in_Ethiopia "Urbanization in Ethiopia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/AddisView.jpg/250px-AddisView.jpg)](/wiki/File:AddisView.jpg)

[Sheger Park](/wiki/Sheger_Park "Sheger Park") is the largest park in the country, hosting hundreds of thousands of visitors a year since its opening.

Population growth, migration, and urbanization are all straining both governments' and ecosystems' capacity to provide people with basic services.[[282]](#cite_note-285) Urbanization has steadily been increasing in Ethiopia, with two periods of significantly rapid growth. First, in 1936–1941 during the Italian occupation under Mussolini's fascist government, and then from 1967 to 1975 when the populations of urban areas tripled.[[283]](#cite_note-countrystudies1991-286)

In 1936, Italy annexed Ethiopia, building infrastructure to connect major cities, and a dam providing power and water.[[284]](#cite_note-Accessed_15_May_2008-287) This, along with the influx of Italians and labourers, was the major cause of rapid growth during this period. The second period of growth was from 1967 to 1975, when rural populations migrated to towns seeking work and better living conditions.[[283]](#cite_note-countrystudies1991-286)

This pattern slowed due to the 1975 Land Reform program instituted by the government, which provided incentives for people to stay in rural areas. As people moved from rural areas to the cities, there were fewer people to grow food for the population. The Land Reform Act was meant to increase agriculture since food production was not keeping up with population growth over the period of 1970–1983. This program encouraged the formation of peasant associations, large villages based on agriculture. The legislation did lead to an increase in food production, although there is debate over the cause; it may be related to weather conditions more than the reform.[[285]](#cite_note-Anderson,_Frank_1991._Pages_159-175-288) Urban populations have continued to grow with an 8.1% increase from 1975 to 2000.
