As of at least 2024, Ethiopia is one of the most rapidly urbanizing countries in the world, although its population is still largely rural.[[287]](#cite_note-:Curtis&Klaus-290)

#### Rural and urban life

Main article: [Rural flight in Ethiopia](/wiki/Rural_flight_in_Ethiopia "Rural flight in Ethiopia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Addis_Ababa_sky_view.jpg/250px-Addis_Ababa_sky_view.jpg)](/wiki/File:Addis_Ababa_sky_view.jpg)

Over 6 million residents live in [Addis Ababa](/wiki/Addis_Ababa "Addis Ababa"), the financial epicenter of the country.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Elders_at_health_worker_training_%285762508115%29.jpg/250px-Elders_at_health_worker_training_%285762508115%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Elders_at_health_worker_training_(5762508115).jpg)

A group of men gather in a rural village for health training.

Migration to urban areas is usually motivated by the hope of better lives. In peasant associations, daily life is a struggle to survive. About 16% of the population in Ethiopia lives on less than one dollar per day (2008). Only 65% of rural households in Ethiopia consume the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization")'s (WHO's) minimum standard of food per day (2,200 kilocalories), with 42% of children under five years old being underweight.[[288]](#cite_note-Crawley-291)

Most poor families (75%) share their sleeping quarters with livestock, and 40% of children sleep on the floor, where nighttime temperatures average 5 degrees Celsius in the cold season.[[288]](#cite_note-Crawley-291) The average family size is six or seven, living in a 30 square metre mud and thatch hut, with less than two hectares of land to cultivate.[[288]](#cite_note-Crawley-291)

The peasant associations face a cycle of poverty. Since the landholdings are so small, farmers cannot allow the land to lie fallow, which reduces soil fertility.[[288]](#cite_note-Crawley-291) This [land degradation](/wiki/Land_degradation "Land degradation") reduces the production of fodder for livestock, which causes low milk yields.[[288]](#cite_note-Crawley-291) Since the community burns livestock manure as fuel, rather than plowing the nutrients back into the land, the crop production is reduced.[[288]](#cite_note-Crawley-291) The low productivity of agriculture leads to inadequate incomes for farmers, hunger, malnutrition and disease. These unhealthy farmers have difficulty working the land and the productivity drops further.[[288]](#cite_note-Crawley-291)

Although conditions are drastically better in cities, all of Ethiopia suffers from poverty and [poor sanitation](/wiki/Water_supply_and_sanitation_in_Ethiopia "Water supply and sanitation in Ethiopia"). However, poverty in Ethiopia fell from 44% to 29.6% during 2000–2011, according to the World Bank.[[289]](#cite_note-292) In the capital city of Addis Ababa, 55% of the population used to live in slums.[[284]](#cite_note-Accessed_15_May_2008-287) Now, however, a construction boom in both the private and the public sector has led to a dramatic improvement in living standards in major cities, particularly in Addis Ababa. Notably, government-built condominium housing complexes have sprung up throughout the city, benefiting close to 600,000 individuals.[[290]](#cite_note-293) Sanitation is the most pressing need in the city, with most of the population lacking access to waste treatment facilities. This contributes to the spread of illness through unhealthy water.[[284]](#cite_note-Accessed_15_May_2008-287)

Despite the urban living conditions, residents of Addis Ababa are more financially secure than those living in peasant associations, owing to their increased educational and economic opportunities. Unlike rural children, 69% of urban children are enrolled in primary school, and 35% are eligible to attend secondary school.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*][[284]](#cite_note-Accessed_15_May_2008-287) Addis Ababa has its own [university](/wiki/Addis_Ababa_University "Addis Ababa University") as well as its own [secondary schools](/wiki/Secondary_school "Secondary school"). The literacy rate in Addis Ababa is 82% as of 2008.[[284]](#cite_note-Accessed_15_May_2008-287)

Many NGOs (non-governmental organizations) are working to solve this problem; however, most are far apart, uncoordinated, and working in isolation. The Sub-Saharan Africa NGO Consortium is attempting to coordinate efforts.

### Languages

Main article: [Languages of Ethiopia](/wiki/Languages_of_Ethiopia "Languages of Ethiopia")

[Languages of Ethiopia](/wiki/Languages_of_Ethiopia "Languages of Ethiopia") as of 2007[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ethiopia&action=edit) Census:[[6]](#cite_note-2007Census-7)

1. [Oromo](/wiki/Oromo_language "Oromo language") (33.8%)
2. [Amharic](/wiki/Amharic "Amharic") (29.3%)
3. [Somali](/wiki/Somali_language "Somali language") (6.20%)
4. [Tigrinya](/wiki/Tigrinya_language "Tigrinya language") (5.90%)
5. [Sidamo](/wiki/Sidamo_language "Sidamo language") (4.00%)
6. [Wolaytta](/wiki/Wolaytta_language "Wolaytta language") (2.20%)
7. [Gurage](/wiki/Gurage_languages "Gurage languages") (2.00%)
8. [Afar](/wiki/Afar_language "Afar language") (1.70%)
9. [Hadiyya](/wiki/Hadiyya_language "Hadiyya language") (1.70%)
10. [Gamo](/wiki/Gamo-Gofa-Dawro_language "Gamo-Gofa-Dawro language") (1.50%)
11. [Other languages](/wiki/Languages_of_Ethiopia#Overview "Languages of Ethiopia") (11.7%)

According to [Glottolog](/wiki/Glottolog "Glottolog"), there are 109 languages spoken in Ethiopia, while *[Ethnologue](/wiki/Ethnologue "Ethnologue")* lists 90 individual languages spoken in the country.[[291]](#cite_note-294)[[292]](#cite_note-Ethnologue-295) Most people in the country speak [Afroasiatic languages](/wiki/Afroasiatic_languages "Afroasiatic languages") of the [Cushitic](/wiki/Cushitic_languages "Cushitic languages") or [Semitic](/wiki/Semitic_languages "Semitic languages") branches. The former includes the [Oromo language](/wiki/Oromo_language "Oromo language"), spoken by the [Oromo](/wiki/Oromo_people "Oromo people"), and [Somali](/wiki/Somali_language "Somali language"), spoken by the [Somalis](/wiki/Somalis "Somalis"); the latter includes [Amharic](/wiki/Amharic "Amharic"), spoken by the [Amhara](/wiki/Amhara_people "Amhara people"), and [Tigrinya](/wiki/Tigrinya_language "Tigrinya language"), spoken by the [Tigrayans](/wiki/Tigrayans "Tigrayans"). Together, these four groups make up about three-quarters of Ethiopia's population. Other Afroasiatic languages with a significant number of speakers include the Cushitic [Sidamo](/wiki/Sidamo_language "Sidamo language"), [Afar](/wiki/Afar_language "Afar language"), [Hadiyya](/wiki/Hadiyya_language "Hadiyya language") and [Agaw languages](/wiki/Agaw_languages "Agaw languages"), as well as the Semitic [Gurage languages](/wiki/Gurage_languages "Gurage languages"), [Harari](/wiki/Harari_language "Harari language"), [Silt'e](/wiki/Silt%27e_language "Silt'e language"), and [Argobba](/wiki/Argobba_language "Argobba language") languages.[[6]](#cite_note-2007Census-7) [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic"), which also belongs to the Afroasiatic family, is likewise spoken in some areas.[[293]](#cite_note-296)

English is the most widely spoken foreign language, the medium of instruction in secondary schools and all tertiary education; federal laws are also published in British English in the [Federal Negarit Gazeta](/wiki/Federal_Negarit_Gazeta "Federal Negarit Gazeta") including the 1995 constitution.[[294]](#cite_note-FDRE-297)

Amharic was the language of primary school instruction, but has been replaced in many areas by regional languages such as Oromo, Somali or Tigrinya.[[295]](#cite_note-Llicuv-298) All languages enjoy equal state recognition in the [1995 Constitution of Ethiopia](/wiki/1995_Constitution_of_Ethiopia "1995 Constitution of Ethiopia").[[142]](#cite_note-Constitution2-145)

#### Script

Ethiopia's principal orthography is the [Ge'ez script](/wiki/Ge%27ez_script "Ge'ez script"). Employed as an [abugida](/wiki/Abugida "Abugida") for several of the country's languages, it first came into usage in the 6th and 5th centuries BC as an [abjad](/wiki/Abjad "Abjad") to transcribe the Semitic [Ge'ez language](/wiki/Ge%27ez_language "Ge'ez language").[[296]](#cite_note-299) Ge'ez now serves as the liturgical language of both the [Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo](/wiki/Ethiopian_Orthodox_Tewahedo_Church "Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church") and [Eritrean Orthodox Tewahedo](/wiki/Eritrean_Orthodox_Tewahedo_Church "Eritrean Orthodox Tewahedo Church") Churches. During the 1980s, the Ethiopic character set was computerized. It is today part of the [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") standard as Ethiopic, [Ethiopic Extended](/wiki/Ethiopic_Extended "Ethiopic Extended"), [Ethiopic Supplement](/wiki/Ethiopic_Supplement "Ethiopic Supplement") and [Ethiopic Extended-A](/wiki/Ethiopic_Extended-A "Ethiopic Extended-A").

Other [writing systems](/wiki/Writing_system "Writing system") have also been used over the years by different Ethiopian communities. The latter include [Bakri Sapalo](/wiki/Bakri_Sapalo "Bakri Sapalo")'s script for [Oromo](/wiki/Oromo_language "Oromo language").[[297]](#cite_note-HH-551-300)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Ethiopia](/wiki/Religion_in_Ethiopia "Religion in Ethiopia")

Religions in Ethiopia (ARDA 2020 est.):[[298]](#cite_note-301)

1. [Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity") (37.5%)
2. [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") (20.4%)
3. [Other Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") (2.96%)
4. [Sunni](/wiki/Sunni "Sunni") (32.5%)
5. [Shia](/wiki/Shia "Shia") (0.10%)
6. [Ethnic Religion](/wiki/African_Traditional_Religion "African Traditional Religion") (6.41%)
7. [None](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion") (0.10%)
8. Other (0.15%)

According to the 2007 National Census, Christians make up 62.8% of the country's population, Muslims 33.9%, practitioners of traditional faiths 2.6%, and other religions 0.6%.[[6]](#cite_note-2007Census-7) The ratio of the Christian to Muslim population has largely remained stable when compared to previous censuses conducted decades ago.[[299]](#cite_note-bx-302) Sunnis form the majority of Muslims with [non-denominational Muslims](/wiki/Non-denominational_Muslim "Non-denominational Muslim") being the second largest group of Muslims, and [the Shia](/wiki/Shia_Islam "Shia Islam") are a minority. Sunnis are largely [Shafi'is](/wiki/Shafi%27i "Shafi'i") or [Salafis](/wiki/Salafi_movement "Salafi movement"); there are also many [Sufis](/wiki/Sufism "Sufism") there.[[300]](#cite_note-303)

Ethiopia has close historical ties with all three of the world's major [Abrahamic religions](/wiki/Abrahamic_religions "Abrahamic religions"). In the 4th century, the Ethiopian empire was one of the first in the world to officially adopt Christianity as the state religion. As a result of the resolutions of the [Council of Chalcedon](/wiki/Council_of_Chalcedon "Council of Chalcedon"), in 451 the [Miaphysites](/wiki/Miaphysitism "Miaphysitism"), which included the vast majority of Christians in Egypt and Ethiopia, were accused of [monophysitism](/wiki/Monophysitism "Monophysitism") and designated as heretics under the common name of [Coptic Christianity](/wiki/Coptic_Orthodox_Church_of_Alexandria "Coptic Orthodox Church of Alexandria") (see [Oriental Orthodoxy](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodoxy "Oriental Orthodoxy")).[[301]](#cite_note-first_seven-304)

The [Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church](/wiki/Ethiopian_Orthodox_Tewahedo_Church "Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church") is part of [Oriental Orthodoxy](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodoxy "Oriental Orthodoxy"). It is by far the largest Christian denomination, although a number of [P'ent'ay](/wiki/P%27ent%27ay "P'ent'ay") ([Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism")) churches have recently gained ground. Since 1930, a relatively small [Ethiopian Catholic Church](/wiki/Ethiopian_Catholic_Church "Ethiopian Catholic Church") has existed in [full communion](/wiki/Full_communion "Full communion") with Rome, with adherents making up less than 1% of the total population.[[299]](#cite_note-bx-302)

[Islam in Ethiopia](/wiki/Islam_in_Ethiopia "Islam in Ethiopia") dates back to the founding of the religion in 622 when a group of Muslims were counselled by [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad") to escape persecution in [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca "Mecca"). The disciples subsequently [migrated to Abyssinia](/wiki/Migrated_to_Abyssinia "Migrated to Abyssinia") via modern-day Eritrea, which was at the time ruled by [Ashama ibn-Abjar](/wiki/Ashama_ibn-Abjar "Ashama ibn-Abjar"), a pious Christian emperor.[[302]](#cite_note-Ofcansky-305)

### Health

Main articles: [Health in Ethiopia](/wiki/Health_in_Ethiopia "Health in Ethiopia") and [Healthcare in Ethiopia](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Ethiopia "Healthcare in Ethiopia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Child_mortality_in_Sub-Saharan_Africa_and_Ethiopia.svg/250px-Child_mortality_in_Sub-Saharan_Africa_and_Ethiopia.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Child_mortality_in_Sub-Saharan_Africa_and_Ethiopia.svg)

Declining child mortality in Sub-Saharan Africa and Ethiopia since 1950

Only a minority of Ethiopians are born in hospitals, while most are born in rural households. Those who are expected to give birth at home have elderly women serve as midwives who assist with the delivery.[[303]](#cite_note-306) The "WHO estimates that a majority of maternal fatalities and disabilities could be prevented if deliveries were to take place at well-equipped health centres, with adequately trained staff".[[304]](#cite_note-307) [Birth rates](/wiki/Birth_rate "Birth rate"), [infant mortality rates](/wiki/Infant_mortality_rate "Infant mortality rate"), and [death rates](/wiki/Death_rate "Death rate") are lower in cities than in rural areas due to better access to education, medicines, and hospitals.[[284]](#cite_note-Accessed_15_May_2008-287) Life expectancy is better in cities compared to rural areas, but there have been significant improvements witnessed throughout the country as of 2016, the average Ethiopian living to be 62.2 years old, according to a [UNDP](/wiki/UNDP "UNDP") report.[[305]](#cite_note-308) Despite sanitation being a problem, use of improved water sources is also on the rise; 81% in cities compared to 11% in rural areas.

Ethiopia's main health problems are said to be communicable (contagious) diseases worsened by poor sanitation and [malnutrition](/wiki/Malnutrition "Malnutrition"). Over 58 million people (nearly half the population) do not have access to clean water as of 2023.[[306]](#cite_note-309) These problems are exacerbated by the shortage of trained doctors and nurses and health facilities.[[307]](#cite_note-310) The [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization")'s 2006 World Health Report gives a figure of 1,936 physicians (for 2003), which comes to about 2.6 per 100,000.[[308]](#cite_note-311)

The National Mental Health Strategy, published in 2012, introduced the development of policy designed to improve mental health care in Ethiopia. This strategy mandated that mental health be integrated into the primary health care system.[[309]](#cite_note-312) However, the success of the National Mental Health Strategy has been limited. For example, the burden of depression is estimated to have increased 34.2% from 2007 to 2017.[[310]](#cite_note-313) Furthermore, the prevalence of stigmatizing attitudes, inadequate leadership and co-ordination of efforts, as well as a lack of mental health awareness in the general population, all remain as obstacles to successful mental health care.[[311]](#cite_note-314)

In the 2024 Global Hunger Index (GHI), Ethiopia ranks 102nd out of 127 countries and has a score of 26.2, which indicates a serious level of hunger.[[312]](#cite_note-315)

### Education

Main article: [Education in Ethiopia](/wiki/Education_in_Ethiopia "Education in Ethiopia")

See also: [Higher education in Ethiopia](/wiki/Higher_education_in_Ethiopia "Higher education in Ethiopia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Parco_dell%27universit%C3%A0_di_addis_abeba%2C_09.jpg/250px-Parco_dell%27universit%C3%A0_di_addis_abeba%2C_09.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parco_dell%27universit%C3%A0_di_addis_abeba,_09.jpg)

Entrance of [Addis Ababa University](/wiki/Addis_Ababa_University "Addis Ababa University")

The current system follows school expansion schemes which are very similar to the system in the rural areas during the 1980s, with an addition of deeper regionalization, providing rural education in students' own languages starting at the elementary level, and with more budgetary financing allocated to the education sector. Public education is free at primary levels and usually offers between age 7 and 12. The sequence of general education in Ethiopia is six years of primary school, then four years of lower secondary school followed by two years of higher secondary school.[[313]](#cite_note-316)

The Ethiopian education is governed by the [Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_(Ethiopia) "Ministry of Education (Ethiopia)") and its cycle consists of a 4+4+2+2 system; elementary education consists of eight years, divided into two cycles of four years, and four years of secondary education, divided into two stages of two years.[[314]](#cite_note-317) National exams are conducted by the [National Education Assessment and Examination Agency](/wiki/National_Educational_Assessment_and_Examination_Agency "National Educational Assessment and Examination Agency") (NEAEA). Since 2018, there are two national exams: the [Ethiopian General Secondary Education Certificate Examination](/wiki/Ethiopian_General_Secondary_Education_Certificate_Examination "Ethiopian General Secondary Education Certificate Examination") (EGSECE), also known as Grade 10 national exam and Grade 12 national exam.[[315]](#cite_note-318)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Top_floor_of_the_first_science_museum_in_Ethiopia.jpg/250px-Top_floor_of_the_first_science_museum_in_Ethiopia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Top_floor_of_the_first_science_museum_in_Ethiopia.jpg)

The [Addis Ababa Science Museum of Art and Science](/wiki/Ethiopia_Museum_of_Art_and_Science "Ethiopia Museum of Art and Science") serves as the country's biggest hub which showcases the country's newest innovations.

As of 2022, there are 83 universities, 42 public universities, and more than 35 higher education institutions. Foreign students constitute 16,305 in higher education level. The overall number of tertiary students in both public and private institutions exploded by more than 2,000 percent, from 34,000 in 1991 to 757,000 in 2014, per UIS data.[[316]](#cite_note-stategov-319)[[317]](#cite_note-320) Access to education in Ethiopia has improved significantly. Approximately three million people were in primary school in 1994–95 but by 2008–09, primary enrolment had risen to 15.5 million – an increase of over 500%.[[318]](#cite_note-321) In 2013–14, Ethiopia had witnessed a significant boost in gross enrolment across all regions.[[319]](#cite_note-322) The national GER was 104.8% for boys, 97.8% for girls and 101.3% across both sexes.[[320]](#cite_note-323)

The literacy rate has increased in recent years: according to the 1994 census, the literacy rate in Ethiopia was 23.4%.[[292]](#cite_note-Ethnologue-295) In 2007 it was estimated to be 39% (male 49.1% and female 28.9%).[[321]](#cite_note-324) A report by [UNDP](/wiki/UNDP "UNDP") in 2011 showed that the literacy rate in Ethiopia was 46.7%. The same report also indicated that the female literacy rate had increased from 27 to 39 per cent from 2004 to 2011, and the male literacy rate had increased from 49 to 59 per cent over the same period for persons 10 years and older.[[322]](#cite_note-325) By 2015, the literacy rate had further increased, to 49.1% (57.2% male and 41.1% female).[[323]](#cite_note-326)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Ethiopia](/wiki/Culture_of_Ethiopia "Culture of Ethiopia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Ethiopia_IMG_5782_Addis_Abeba_%2825052967317%29.jpg/250px-Ethiopia_IMG_5782_Addis_Abeba_%2825052967317%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ethiopia_IMG_5782_Addis_Abeba_(25052967317).jpg)

Cultural performance in Addis Ababa

The [culture of Ethiopia](/wiki/Culture_of_Ethiopia "Culture of Ethiopia") is diverse, consisting of the local population, an interaction of Semitic, Cushitic and less populous Nilo-Saharan speaking people, which is evolved from first millennium BC. The dominant Semitic-speaking groups such as [Tigrayans](/wiki/Tigrayans "Tigrayans"), [Amharas](/wiki/Amhara_people "Amhara people") and [Gurage](/wiki/Gurage_people "Gurage people"), are distinguished by their feudal tradition, hierarchical structure and agrarian life. This is rooted partly from [South Arabia](/wiki/South_Arabia "South Arabia") as a result of back migration, while the southern Cushitic ([Oromo](/wiki/Oromo_people "Oromo people") and [Somali](/wiki/Somalis "Somalis")) are strong adherents to [egalitarianism](/wiki/Egalitarianism "Egalitarianism") and pastoral life. Other traditions from the [Kafficho](/wiki/Kafficho_people "Kafficho people"), [Sidamo](/wiki/Sidama_people "Sidama people"), and [Afar](/wiki/Afar_people "Afar people") peoples are also present.[[324]](#cite_note-327)

### Holidays

Main article: [Public holidays in Ethiopia](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Ethiopia "Public holidays in Ethiopia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Ethiopian_New_Year_Landscape._Photo_Taken_on_September_11_2010_Ethiopian_Calendar._Ethiopian_indigenous_flower_%22Adey_Abeba%22_Sheeps_seen_grazing.jpg/250px-Ethiopian_New_Year_Landscape._Photo_Taken_on_September_11_2010_Ethiopian_Calendar._Ethiopian_indigenous_flower_%22Adey_Abeba%22_Sheeps_seen_grazing.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ethiopian_New_Year_Landscape._Photo_Taken_on_September_11_2010_Ethiopian_Calendar._Ethiopian_indigenous_flower_%22Adey_Abeba%22_Sheeps_seen_grazing.jpg)

The Ethiopian New Year or [Enkutatash](/wiki/Enkutatash "Enkutatash") is celebrated with the [adey abeba](/wiki/Bidens_macroptera "Bidens macroptera") flower symbolising a new beginning, each year across the country.

Ethiopia has 6 patriotic and public holidays and 9 major religious holidays given government recognition. The year (Gregorian calendar) starts with [Ethiopian Christmas](/wiki/Ethiopian_Christmas "Ethiopian Christmas") on January 7, it also has [Epiphany](/wiki/Timkat "Timkat") or Timkat on January 19 or 20 depending on if the year is a leap, which celebrates the baptism of [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") by [John the Baptist](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist"). Timkat is recognized by the United Nations as part of its "[Intangible Cultural Heritage Lists](/wiki/UNESCO_Intangible_Cultural_Heritage_Lists "UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage Lists")" which are designated for important cultural and religious holidays.[[325]](#cite_note-328)[[326]](#cite_note-329) Three holidays are movable like [Ramadan](/wiki/Ramadan "Ramadan"), [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday") and [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter") of which two are in spring and Ramadan in Islamic celebrations. Other holidays include [Mawlid](/wiki/Mawlid "Mawlid") which begins on the 12th or 17th on the Islamic calendar depending on which Muslim denomination is the celebrator, nonetheless due to the fact that the majority of Ethiopian Muslims are Sunni it is thus celebrated on the 12th day on the third month on the Islamic calendar which is in September. Other include [Eid al-Fitr](/wiki/Eid_al-Fitr "Eid al-Fitr") and [Eid al-Adha](/wiki/Eid_al-Adha "Eid al-Adha") for other Islamic holidays whilst for Christians, the finding of the true cross or otherwise is called [Meskel](/wiki/Meskel "Meskel").[[327]](#cite_note-330)[[328]](#cite_note-:05-331)[[329]](#cite_note-332)

For public holidays the first of which falls on the calendar is [Adwa Victory Day](/wiki/Adwa_Victory_Day "Adwa Victory Day") on 2 March, commemorating [Emperor Menelik II](/wiki/Menelik_II "Menelik II")'s victory over the attempted but failed colonization efforts of Italy in 1896. Others follow, such as the [International Workers' Day](/wiki/International_Workers%27_Day "International Workers' Day") on the 1st of May, the [Ethiopian Patriots' Victory Day](/wiki/Ethiopian_Patriots%27_Victory_Day "Ethiopian Patriots' Victory Day") for celebrating the return of Emperor Haile Selassie I and thus the liberation of fascist Italian occupation on the 5th of May and the [Downfall of the Derg](/wiki/Downfall_of_the_Derg_(holiday) "Downfall of the Derg (holiday)") on the 28th of May, a recent holiday promoted to a public status by the ruling government after 1991. And the most prominent of public holidays is [Enkutatash](/wiki/Enkutatash "Enkutatash") which is the largest, celebrating the [Ethiopian new year](/wiki/Enkutatash "Enkutatash") on September 11 or 12, which is approximately 7–8 years behind the rest of the world, depending on if the year is a leap, and thus behind ultimately the Georgian calendar by several years. [Defense Day](/wiki/Defense_Day_(Ethiopia) "Defense Day (Ethiopia)") also exists for celebrating service men on the 26th of October the last holiday in the Gregorian calendar.[[330]](#cite_note-333)

Nonetheless other festivals and holidays like the honoring of Saint Yohannes and others are celebrated across the country including [Irrecha](/wiki/Irreechaa "Irreechaa") celebrated by the [Oromo community](/wiki/Oromo_people "Oromo people").[[331]](#cite_note-334)[[332]](#cite_note-335)

### Art and architecture

Main articles: [Ethiopian art](/wiki/Ethiopian_art "Ethiopian art") and [Architecture of Ethiopia](/wiki/Architecture_of_Ethiopia "Architecture of Ethiopia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/The_Last_Judgement_by_Afewerk_Tekle_at_St._George%27s_Cathedral.jpg/250px-The_Last_Judgement_by_Afewerk_Tekle_at_St._George%27s_Cathedral.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Last_Judgement_by_Afewerk_Tekle_at_St._George%27s_Cathedral.jpg)

[Afewerk Tekle](/wiki/Afewerk_Tekle "Afewerk Tekle")'s "The Last Judgment" at [St. George's Capel](/wiki/St._George%27s_Cathedral,_Addis_Ababa "St. George's Cathedral, Addis Ababa")

[Arts of Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopian_art "Ethiopian art") were largely influenced by Christian iconography throughout much of its history. This consisted of [illuminated manuscripts](/wiki/Illuminated_manuscripts "Illuminated manuscripts"), [painting](/wiki/Painting "Painting"), [crosses](/wiki/Ethiopian_cross "Ethiopian cross"), [icons](/wiki/Icon "Icon") and other [metalwork](/wiki/Metalwork "Metalwork") such as crowns. Most historical arts were commissioned by the Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church, the state religion for a millennium. The earlier Aksumite period arts were stone carvings as evidenced in their stelae, though there is no surviving Christian art from this era. As Christianity was introduced, its iconography was partly influenced by [Byzantine art](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"). Most remaining arts beyond the early modern period were ruined as a result of invasion of the [Adal Sultanate](/wiki/Adal_Sultanate "Adal Sultanate") in the [Ethiopian Highlands](/wiki/Ethiopian_Highlands "Ethiopian Highlands"), but were revived by Catholic emissaries.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The [Western](/wiki/Western_art "Western art") intervention in Ethiopian art began in the 20th century, while also maintaining traditional Ethiopian character. Some notable contemporary Ethiopian artists include [Afewerk Tekle](/wiki/Afewerk_Tekle "Afewerk Tekle"), [Lemma Guya](/wiki/Lemma_Guya "Lemma Guya"), [Martha Nasibù](/wiki/Martha_Nasib%C3%B9 "Martha Nasibù"), [Ale Felege Selam](/wiki/Ale_Felege_Selam "Ale Felege Selam") and others.[[333]](#cite_note-336)[[334]](#cite_note-337)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Stelae%2C_Aksum%2C_Ethiopia_%287158408756%29.jpg/250px-Stelae%2C_Aksum%2C_Ethiopia_%287158408756%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stelae,_Aksum,_Ethiopia_(7158408756).jpg)

The [Aksumite Steles](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Aksum "Kingdom of Aksum") served as official markers for the [Emperors of Ethiopia](/wiki/Emperor_of_Ethiopia "Emperor of Ethiopia") during the time of the [Aksumite Empire](/wiki/Aksumite_Empire "Aksumite Empire").

Ethiopian architecture like the "Bete Medhane Alem" or "House of our Saviour" is one of the 12 churches in [Lalibela](/wiki/Lalibela "Lalibela") built under Emperor [Lalibela I](/wiki/Lalibela_(Emperor_of_Ethiopia) "Lalibela (Emperor of Ethiopia)"). [Emperor Lalibela I](/wiki/Gebre_Meskel_Lalibela "Gebre Meskel Lalibela") commissioned large portions of the Lalibela church complex promptly named after him. This was largely attested to the inspiration for Ethiopia during the medieval times due to blockage from Jerusalem by Muslim conquests to replicate its own form of a "new Jerusalem" at a national level. Perhaps one of the most notable architectures in antiquity was founded during the Dʿmt period. [Ashlar masonry](/wiki/Ashlar_masonry "Ashlar masonry") was an archetype of [South Arabian](/wiki/South_Arabia "South Arabia") architecture with the most architectural structure similarity.[[335]](#cite_note-338)[[336]](#cite_note-339)

The Aksumite continued to flourish its architecture around the 4th century CE. Aksumite stelae commonly used single block and rocks. The Tomb of the False Door built for Aksumite emperors used monolithic style.[[337]](#cite_note-340) The [Lalibela](/wiki/Lalibela "Lalibela") civilization was largely of Aksumite influence, but the layer of stones or wood is quite different for some dwellings.[[338]](#cite_note-341)

In the Gondarine period, the architecture of Ethiopia was influenced by Baroque, Arab, Turkish and Gujarati Indian styles independently taught by Portuguese emissaries in the 16th and 17th centuries.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Examples include the imperial fortress [Fasil Ghebbi](/wiki/Fasil_Ghebbi "Fasil Ghebbi"), which is influenced by a mix of these styles. The medieval architecture also affected the later 19th- and 20th-century era.[[339]](#cite_note-342)

### Literature

Main articles: [Ethiopian literature](/wiki/Ethiopian_literature "Ethiopian literature") and [Ethiopian philosophy](/wiki/Ethiopian_philosophy "Ethiopian philosophy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Baalu_Girma.jpg/250px-Baalu_Girma.jpg)](/wiki/File:Baalu_Girma.jpg)

[Baalu Girma](/wiki/Baalu_Girma "Baalu Girma") (1939–1984), novelist and critic; author of *[The End](/wiki/Oromay "Oromay")*[[340]](#cite_note-343)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Haddis_Alemayehu.png/250px-Haddis_Alemayehu.png)](/wiki/File:Haddis_Alemayehu.png)

[Haddis Alemayehu](/wiki/Haddis_Alemayehu "Haddis Alemayehu") (1910–2003), foreign minister and novelist; author of *[Love to the Grave](/wiki/Love_to_the_Grave "Love to the Grave")*[[341]](#cite_note-344)

[Ethiopian literature](/wiki/Ethiopian_literature "Ethiopian literature") traces back to the Aksumite period in the 4th century, mostly religious motifs. In royal inscriptions, it employed both [Ge'ez](/wiki/Ge%CA%BDez_script "Geʽez script") and [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") language, but the latter was discontinued in 350.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Unlike most Sub-Saharan African countries, Ethiopia has an ancient distinct language, the Ge'ez, which dominated political and educational aspects. In spite of the current political instability in the country endangering cultural heritage of these works, preservation has improved in recent years.[[342]](#cite_note-:4-345)

During and the approximate time of the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") composers such as [Abba Gorgoryos](/wiki/Abba_Gorgoryos "Abba Gorgoryos") and [Giyorgis of Segla](/wiki/Giyorgis_of_Segla "Giyorgis of Segla") have influenced Ethiopian languages such as writing one of the earliest instances of [Amharic](/wiki/Amharic "Amharic") and dictionaries of Ge'ez and such. Also encompassing religious hymns and doctrine justifications regarding issues in the Ethiopian Orthodox church and practices.[[343]](#cite_note-HeldmanSeyon-346)[[344]](#cite_note-:02-347)

The Ethiopian literary works mostly consisted of handwritten [codex](/wiki/Codex "Codex") (branna, or ብራና in Amharic). It is prepared by gathering parchment leaves and sewing to stick together. The codex size varies considerably depending on volumes and preparation. For example, pocket size codex lengthens 45 cm, which is heavier in weight. Historians speculated that archaic codex existed in Ethiopia. Today manuscripts resembling primitive codex are still evident for existence where parchment leaves are convenient for writing.[[342]](#cite_note-:4-345)

Another notable writing book is protective (or magic) [scroll](/wiki/Scroll "Scroll"), serving as written amulet. Some of these were intended for magical purpose, for example *ketab* is used for magical defence. Scrolls were typically produced by *[debtera](/wiki/Debtera "Debtera")*, non-ordained clergy expertise on [exorcism](/wiki/Exorcism "Exorcism") and healings. About 30 cm scroll is portable whereas 2 cm is often unrolled and hanged to the walls of houses. Scrolls emulating original medium of Ethiopia literature is highly disputed, where there is overwhelming evidence that Ge'ez language books were written in codex. In lesser, Ethiopia used accordion books (called *sensul*) which were dated to late 15th or 16th century, made up of folded parchment paper, with or without cover. Those books usually contain pictorial representation of life and death of religious figures, or significant texts have also juxtaposed.[[342]](#cite_note-:4-345)

[Baalu Girma](/wiki/Baalu_Girma "Baalu Girma") and [Haddis Alemayehu](/wiki/Haddis_Alemayehu "Haddis Alemayehu") have been noted as the most influential novelist in Ethiopian history. Girma giving a critique of the communist government in the 80s in Ethiopia in his works. Haddis Alemayehu giving rise to one of the first examinations of [realism](/wiki/Realism_(international_relations) "Realism (international relations)") and a [romance](/wiki/Romance_novel "Romance novel") tragedy in his works of novel and having influenced the sphere of Ethiopian intellectual community.[[345]](#cite_note-348)[[346]](#cite_note-349)

Ethiopia is highly popularized in [poetry](/wiki/Poetry "Poetry"). Most poets recount past events, social unrests, poverty and famine. *[Qene](/wiki/Qene "Qene")* is the most used element of Ethiopian poetry – regarded as a form of Amharic poetry, though the term generally refers to any poems. True qene requires advanced ingenious mindset. By providing two metaphorical words, i.e. one with obvious clues and the other is too convoluted conundrum, one must answer parallel meanings. Thus, this is called *sem ena work* (gold and wax).[[347]](#cite_note-:9-350) The most notable poets are [Tsegaye Gebre-Medhin](/wiki/Tsegaye_Gabre-Medhin "Tsegaye Gabre-Medhin"),[[348]](#cite_note-351) [Kebede Michael](/wiki/Kebede_Michael "Kebede Michael") and [Mengistu Lemma](/wiki/Mengistu_Lemma "Mengistu Lemma").[[349]](#cite_note-352)

Ethiopian philosophy has been superlatively prolific since ancient times in Africa, though offset of [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_philosophy "Ancient Greek philosophy") and [Patristic](/wiki/Patristic "Patristic") philosophy. The best known philosophical revival was in the early modern period figures such as [Zera Yacob](/wiki/Zera_Yacob_(philosopher) "Zera Yacob (philosopher)") (1599–1692) and his student [Walda Heywat](/wiki/Walda_Heywat "Walda Heywat"), who wrote *[Hatata](/wiki/Hatata "Hatata")* (*Inquiry*) in 1667 as an argument for the [existence of God](/wiki/Existence_of_God "Existence of God").

### Music

Main article: [Music of Ethiopia](/wiki/Music_of_Ethiopia "Music of Ethiopia")

See also: [Ethiopian Golden Age of music](/wiki/Ethiopian_Golden_Age_of_music "Ethiopian Golden Age of music")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Yared.jpg/250px-Yared.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yared.jpg)

[Yared](/wiki/Yared "Yared") in a piece of 15th century Ethiopian sacred art holding a mequamia (prayer stick)

The [music of Ethiopia](/wiki/Music_of_Ethiopia "Music of Ethiopia") is extremely diverse, with each of the country's 80 ethnic groups being associated with unique sounds. Ethiopian music uses a distinct [modal system](/wiki/Musical_mode "Musical mode") that is [pentatonic](/wiki/Pentatonic_scale "Pentatonic scale"), with characteristically long intervals between some notes. As with many other aspects of Ethiopian culture and tradition, tastes in music and lyrics are strongly linked with those in neighbouring Eritrea, Somalia, Djibouti, and Sudan.[[350]](#cite_note-Abdullahi-353)[[351]](#cite_note-Tekle-354) Traditional singing in Ethiopia presents diverse styles of [polyphony](/wiki/Polyphony "Polyphony") ([heterophony](/wiki/Heterophony "Heterophony"), [drone](/wiki/Drone_(music) "Drone (music)"), imitation, and [counterpoint](/wiki/Counterpoint "Counterpoint")). Traditionally, lyricism in Ethiopian song writing is strongly associated with views of patriotism or national pride, romance, friendship, and a unique type of memoire known as *[tizita](/wiki/Tizita "Tizita")*.

Saint [Yared](/wiki/Yared "Yared"), a 6th-century Aksumite composer, is widely regarded as the forerunner of traditional music of Eritrea and Ethiopia, creating liturgical music of the Ethiopian and [Eritrean Orthodox Tewahedo Church](/wiki/Eritrean_Orthodox_Tewahedo_Church "Eritrean Orthodox Tewahedo Church").[[352]](#cite_note-355)

Modern music is traced back to the reign of Emperor Haile Selassie, where 40 Armenian orphans called [Arba Lijoch](/wiki/Arba_Lijoch "Arba Lijoch") arrived from [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") to Addis Ababa. By 1924, the band was almost established as orchestral; but after World War II, several similar bands emerged such as Imperial Bodyguard Band, Army Band, and Police Band.[[353]](#cite_note-356)

In the 1960s and 1970s, traditional infused modern Ethiopian music was revived in what is known as the "[Golden Age](/wiki/Ethiopian_Golden_Age_of_music "Ethiopian Golden Age of music")". Several notable musical artists emerged thereafter, for example, [Tilahun Gessesse](/wiki/Tilahun_Gessesse "Tilahun Gessesse"), [Alemayehu Eshete](/wiki/Alemayehu_Eshete "Alemayehu Eshete"), [Bizunesh Bekele](/wiki/Bizunesh_Bekele "Bizunesh Bekele"), [Muluken Melesse](/wiki/Muluken_Melesse "Muluken Melesse") and [Mahmoud Ahmed](/wiki/Mahmoud_Ahmed "Mahmoud Ahmed"). It also employed tradition style called *tizita*. During the Derg regime, these artists were prohibited to perform in the country and often forced into exile in North America and Europe, mixing with jazz and funk influences. For example, Roha Band, [Walias Band](/wiki/Walias_Band "Walias Band"), and Ethio Stars. By this time, [Neway Debebe](/wiki/Neway_Debebe "Neway Debebe") was critical of the Derg government.[[347]](#cite_note-:9-350)

Modern music became developed shortly in the 1990s and 2000s. In this period, the most popular artists were [Aster Aweke](/wiki/Aster_Aweke "Aster Aweke"), [Gigi](/wiki/Gigi_(singer) "Gigi (singer)") and [Teddy Afro](/wiki/Teddy_Afro "Teddy Afro"). Ethiopian music further modernized in the next decade, employing electronic type and more popular. DJ [Rophnan](/wiki/Rophnan "Rophnan") was renowned for pioneering EDM after releasing his debut album *Reflection* in 2018.[[347]](#cite_note-:9-350)

### Calendar

Main articles: [Ethiopian calendar](/wiki/Ethiopian_calendar "Ethiopian calendar") and [Oromo calendar](/wiki/Oromo_calendar "Oromo calendar")

Ethiopia has several local calendars. The most widely known is the [Ethiopian calendar](/wiki/Ethiopian_calendar "Ethiopian calendar"), also known as the Ge'ez calendar, and written with the ancient [Ge'ez script](/wiki/Ge%27ez_script "Ge'ez script"), one of the oldest alphabets still in use in the world.[[354]](#cite_note-Wilfpag-357) It is based on the older Alexandrian or [Coptic calendar](/wiki/Coptic_calendar "Coptic calendar"), which in turn derives from the [Egyptian calendar](/wiki/Egyptian_calendar "Egyptian calendar"). Like the Coptic calendar, the Ethiopian calendar has twelve months of exactly 30 days each plus five or six [epagomenal](/wiki/Epagomenal "Epagomenal") days, which form a thirteenth month. The Ethiopian months begin on the same days as those of the Coptic calendar, but their names are in Ge'ez.[[355]](#cite_note-358)

Like the [Julian calendar](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar"), the sixth epagomenal day—which in essence is a [leap day](/wiki/Leap_day "Leap day")—is added every four years without exception on 29 August of the Julian calendar, six months before the Julian leap day. Thus, the first day of the Ethiopian year, 1 Mäskäräm, for years between 1901 and 2099 (inclusive), is usually 11 September ([Gregorian](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar")), but falls on 12 September in years before the Gregorian leap year. It is approximately seven years and three months behind the Gregorian calendar because of an alternate calculation in determining the date of the [Annunciation](/wiki/Annunciation "Annunciation") of Jesus.[[356]](#cite_note-auto1-359)

Another calendrical system was developed around 300 BC by the [Oromo people](/wiki/Oromo_people "Oromo people"). A lunar-stellar calendar, this [Oromo calendar](/wiki/Oromo_calendar "Oromo calendar") relies on astronomical observations of the moon in conjunction with seven particular stars or constellations. Oromo months (stars/lunar phases) are *Bittottessa* (Iangulum), *Camsa* (Pleiades), *Bufa* (Aldebarran), *Waxabajjii* (Belletrix), *Obora Gudda* (Central Orion-Saiph), *Obora Dikka* (Sirius), *Birra* (full moon), *Cikawa* (gibbous moon), *Sadasaa* (quarter moon), *Abrasa* (large crescent), *Ammaji* (medium crescent), and *Gurrandala* (small crescent).[[357]](#cite_note-360)

### Media

Main articles: [Communications in Ethiopia](/wiki/Communications_in_Ethiopia "Communications in Ethiopia") and [Media in Ethiopia](/wiki/Media_in_Ethiopia "Media in Ethiopia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Ethiopian_Television.JPG/250px-Ethiopian_Television.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ethiopian_Television.JPG)

The [Ethiopian Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/ETV_(Ethiopia) "ETV (Ethiopia)"), former headquarters in Addis Ababa

The [Ethiopian Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/Ethiopian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Ethiopian Broadcasting Corporation") (EBC), formerly known as ETV, is the [state media](/wiki/State_media "State media"). Radio broadcasting was commenced earlier in 1935 before the television service began in 1962 with assistance of British firm Thomson and Emperor Haile Selassie.[[3]](#cite_note-africanews.com-4) Since 2015, EBC has upgraded its studios with modernized transmission.

[Kana TV](/wiki/Kana_TV "Kana TV") is the most popular TV channel in Ethiopia.[[358]](#cite_note-361) It is mainly known for [dubbing](/wiki/Dubbing_(filmmaking) "Dubbing (filmmaking)") foreign content into [Amharic](/wiki/Amharic "Amharic"). Over several decades, the state television has served as the major mass media until in the late 2000s, when [EBS TV](/wiki/EBS_TV_(Ethiopia) "EBS TV (Ethiopia)") launched as the first private television channel. Moreover, numerous private channels were commenced in 2016, culminating in the growth of privately owned media companies in the country. As an example, [Fana TV](/wiki/Fana_TV "Fana TV") has been the largest TV network since its launch in 2017.

The most widely circulated newspapers in Ethiopia are [Addis Fortune](/wiki/Addis_Fortune "Addis Fortune"), [Capital Ethiopia](/wiki/Capital_Ethiopia "Capital Ethiopia"), [Ethiopian Reporter](/wiki/Ethiopian_Reporter "Ethiopian Reporter"), Addis Zemen (*Amharic*) and [Ethiopian Herald](/wiki/Ethiopian_Herald "Ethiopian Herald").[[359]](#cite_note-362)

The sole internet service provider is the national telecommunications firm [Ethio telecom](/wiki/Ethio_telecom "Ethio telecom"). A large portion of users in the country access the internet through mobile devices.[[360]](#cite_note-:3-363) As of July 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ethiopia&action=edit), there are around 4.29 million people who have internet access at their home as compared to a quarter of a million users a decade before that.[[361]](#cite_note-364) The Ethiopian government has at times intentionally shut down internet service in the country or restricted access to certain social media sites during periods of political unrest. In August 2016, following protest and demonstration in the Oromia Region, all access to the internet was shut down for a period of two days.[[362]](#cite_note-365) In June 2017, the government shut down access to the internet for mobile users during a period that coincided with the administration of university entrance examination. Although the reason for the restriction was not confirmed by the government,[[360]](#cite_note-:3-363) the move was similar to a measure taken during the same period in 2016, after a leak of test questions.[[363]](#cite_note-366)[[364]](#cite_note-367)

### Cinema

Main article: [Cinema of Ethiopia](/wiki/Cinema_of_Ethiopia "Cinema of Ethiopia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Ruth_Negga_by_Gage_Skidmore_3.jpg/250px-Ruth_Negga_by_Gage_Skidmore_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ruth_Negga_by_Gage_Skidmore_3.jpg)

[Ruth Negga](/wiki/Ruth_Negga "Ruth Negga") is an Ethiopian-born actress.

The first cinema was introduced in 1898, three years after the first world film was projected. Cinematic artifacts ascribed by Italian minister [Federico Ciccodicola](/w/index.php?title=Federico_Ciccodicola&action=edit&redlink=1 "Federico Ciccodicola (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Federico_Ciccodicola "it:Federico Ciccodicola")] which then offered to Emperor Menelik II. The early 20th century appearance with spectacle was around 1909 and embraced by documentary or biographical films. *Au de Menilek* was the first film directed by Charles Martel. The first 16mm black-and-white film dedicated to coronation of Emperor [Zewditu](/wiki/Zewditu "Zewditu"), then coronation of Emperor Haile Selassie was filmed. The 1990s saw international booming of Ethiopian films. The most influential people in this era were [Haile Gerima](/wiki/Haile_Gerima "Haile Gerima"), [Salem Mekuria](/wiki/Salem_Mekuria "Salem Mekuria"), Yemane Demissie, and [Teshome Gabriel](/wiki/Teshome_Gabriel "Teshome Gabriel"). Films began modernized in the 2000s and implemented [Amharic](/wiki/Amharic "Amharic") language. The most internationally grossed films are *[Selanchi](/wiki/Selanchi "Selanchi")*, *[Difret](/wiki/Difret "Difret")*, *[Lamb](/wiki/Lamb_(2015_Ethiopian_film) "Lamb (2015 Ethiopian film)")*, *[Prince of Love](/wiki/Prince_of_Love "Prince of Love")* and *[Lambadina](/wiki/Lambadina "Lambadina")*. The modern era saw several reoccurring actors including [Selam Tesfaye](/wiki/Selam_Tesfaye "Selam Tesfaye"), [Fryat Yemane](/wiki/Fryat_Yemane "Fryat Yemane"), [Hanan Tarik](/wiki/Hanan_Tarik "Hanan Tarik"), [Mahder Assefa](/wiki/Mahder_Assefa "Mahder Assefa"), [Amleset Muchie](/wiki/Amleset_Muchie "Amleset Muchie") and [Ruth Negga](/wiki/Ruth_Negga "Ruth Negga").

One of the most prestigious film award is [Gumma Film Awards](/wiki/Gumma_Film_Awards "Gumma Film Awards") held in Addis Ababa. The award, which was started in 2014, broadcast on live television in some stations.[[365]](#cite_note-368) Festivals including [Addis International Film Festival](/wiki/Addis_International_Film_Festival "Addis International Film Festival") and the [Ethiopian International Film Festival](/wiki/Ethiopian_International_Film_Festival "Ethiopian International Film Festival") showcase amateur and professional filmmakers works; the latter being voted by judges. They were established in 2007[[366]](#cite_note-369) and 2005 respectively.[[367]](#cite_note-370)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Ethiopian cuisine](/wiki/Ethiopian_cuisine "Ethiopian cuisine")

See also: [List of Ethiopian dishes and foods](/wiki/List_of_Ethiopian_dishes_and_foods "List of Ethiopian dishes and foods")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Injera_with_eight_kinds_of_stew.jpg/250px-Injera_with_eight_kinds_of_stew.jpg)](/wiki/File:Injera_with_eight_kinds_of_stew.jpg)

This meal consisting of *[injera](/wiki/Injera "Injera")* and several kinds of *[wat](/wiki/Wat_(food) "Wat (food)")* (stew) is typical of Ethiopian cuisine.

The best-known [Ethiopian cuisine](/wiki/Ethiopian_cuisine "Ethiopian cuisine") consists of various types of thick meat [stews](/wiki/Stew "Stew"), known as *[wat](/wiki/Wat_(food) "Wat (food)")* in Ethiopian culture, and vegetable side dishes served on top of [injera](/wiki/Injera "Injera"), a large [sourdough](/wiki/Sourdough "Sourdough") [flatbread](/wiki/Flatbread "Flatbread") made of [teff](/wiki/Teff "Teff") flour. This is not eaten with utensils, but instead the injera is used to scoop up the entrées and side dishes. Almost universally in Ethiopia, it is common to eat from the same dish in the middle of the table with a group of people. It is also a common custom to feed others with one's own hands—a tradition referred to as *gursha*.[[368]](#cite_note-371) Traditional Ethiopian cuisine employs no [pork](/wiki/Pork "Pork"), as it is forbidden in [Ethiopian Orthodox](/wiki/Ethiopian_Orthodox "Ethiopian Orthodox") Christian and Islamic faiths; Ethiopian Orthodox Christians also [fast from meat](/wiki/Fasting_and_abstinence_in_the_Ethiopian_Orthodox_Tewahedo_Church "Fasting and abstinence in the Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church") on Wednesdays, Fridays, and the daysleading up to Easter and Christmas.[[369]](#cite_note-372)

*[Chechebsa](/wiki/Fit-fit#Kitcha_fit-fit "Fit-fit")*, *[marqa](/w/index.php?title=Marqa&action=edit&redlink=1 "Marqa (page does not exist)")*, *[chukko](/w/index.php?title=Chukko&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chukko (page does not exist)")*, *[michirra](/w/index.php?title=Michirra&action=edit&redlink=1 "Michirra (page does not exist)")*, and *[dhanga](/wiki/Dhanga "Dhanga")* are the most popular dishes from the [Oromo people](/wiki/Oromo_people "Oromo people"). [Kitfo](/wiki/Kitfo "Kitfo"), which originated among the [Gurage people](/wiki/Gurage_people "Gurage people"), is one of the country's most popular delicacies. In addition, *[doro wat](/wiki/Wat_(food)#Doro_wat "Wat (food)")* ([Amharic](/wiki/Amharic_language "Amharic language"): ዶሮ ወጥ) and *[tsebehi derho](/w/index.php?title=Tsebehi_derho&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tsebehi derho (page does not exist)")* ([Tigrinya](/wiki/Tigrinya_language "Tigrinya language"): ጽብሒ ድርሆ), are other popular dishes, originating from northwestern Ethiopia.[[370]](#cite_note-373) *[Tihlo](/wiki/Tihlo "Tihlo")* (ጥሕሎ), a type of [dumpling](/wiki/Dumpling "Dumpling") prepared from roasted barley flour and originating from the [Tigray Region](/wiki/Tigray_Region "Tigray Region"), is now very popular in [Amhara](/wiki/Amhara_Region "Amhara Region") and spreading further south.[[371]](#cite_note-374)

### Sport

Main article: [Sport in Ethiopia](/wiki/Sport_in_Ethiopia "Sport in Ethiopia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Abebe_Bikila_1968c.jpg/250px-Abebe_Bikila_1968c.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abebe_Bikila_1968c.jpg)

[Abebe Bikila](/wiki/Abebe_Bikila "Abebe Bikila"), Ethiopia's first [Olympic gold medalist](/wiki/Olympic_gold_medalist "Olympic gold medalist")

The main sports in Ethiopia are [track and field](/wiki/Track_and_field "Track and field") (particularly [long distance running](/wiki/Long_distance_running "Long distance running")) and football. Ethiopian athletes have won many [Olympic](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") gold medals in track and field, most of them in long distance running.[[372]](#cite_note-375) [Abebe Bikila](/wiki/Abebe_Bikila "Abebe Bikila") became the first athlete from a [Sub-Saharan](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa") country to win an Olympic gold medal when he won the [Marathon](/wiki/Marathon "Marathon") at the 1960 Rome Olympic Games in a world record time of 2:15:16.[[373]](#cite_note-376)[[374]](#cite_note-377)

The [Ethiopia national football team](/wiki/Ethiopia_national_football_team "Ethiopia national football team") was one of four founding members of the [Confederation of African Football](/wiki/Confederation_of_African_Football "Confederation of African Football") and won the [Africa Cup of Nations](/wiki/Africa_Cup_of_Nations "Africa Cup of Nations") in [1962](/wiki/1962_African_Cup_of_Nations "1962 African Cup of Nations").[[375]](#cite_note-378) Ethiopia has Sub-Saharan Africa's longest basketball tradition as it established a [national basketball team](/wiki/Ethiopia_national_basketball_team "Ethiopia national basketball team") in 1949.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
